content:
1	---
2	name: blueprint-reviewer
3	description: Whole-blueprint audit. Per-chapter checklist of completeness + correctness plus summaries of which parts are incomplete, which proofs lack detail, whether Lean targets are well-formulated, and whether multi-route strategies have coverage for every route. Also audits STRATEGY.md phases for blueprint coverage gaps and proposes concrete chapter outlines for phases not yet started, so the plan agent can immediately dispatch a blueprint-writer rather than defer.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	mandatory: [plan]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - Dispatch me BEFORE writing any prover objectives or touching Lean files.
11	    The plan agent's job is to make sure the blueprint is complete and
12	    detailed enough first; only then should provers be assigned. A weak
13	    blueprint produces low-quality prover work that the next iter then
14	    has to throw away.
15	  - I am highly recommended every plan phase. The audit warning fires
16	    if I am skipped without a recorded rationale.
17	
18	    **You may skip me this iter when ALL of:**
19	      - no chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/` was edited since my
20	        prior dispatch (check via `git diff --stat HEAD~N
21	        blueprint/src/chapters/` where N spans iters back to my last
22	        run);
23	      - my prior verdict cleared the HARD GATE for all chapters
24	        currently under active prover work;
25	      - no must-fix-this-iter finding from my prior dispatch remains
26	        live (every flagged chapter has either been writer-patched or
27	        dropped from objectives).
28	
29	    Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`
30	    with the one-line rationale. Do NOT skip me when the prior verdict
31	    flagged any chapter `partial | false` and that chapter still feeds
32	    a live prover lane — the HARD GATE depends on a current audit.
33	  - I always read the WHOLE blueprint. Do not pass me a scope-limiting
34	    directive — even when the iteration's focus is narrow, the cross-
35	    chapter view is the point of running me.
36	  - Read my per-chapter checklist and use it to decide which chapters
37	    need a follow-up writer dispatch this iter (consult your catalog
38	    for the blueprint-writing subagent). You do not need to re-read
39	    the chapters yourself; the checklist is your view into them.
40	  - **Act on my unstarted-phase proposals.** My report's
41	    `## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals` section names every
42	    strategy phase with no blueprint coverage and provides a concrete
43	    chapter outline for each. These are not informational — treat
44	    each proposal as a blueprint-writer directive seed. Dispatch a
45	    blueprint-writing subagent for each proposed chapter this iter
46	    (or record an explicit deferral rationale in the iter sidecar).
47	    Do NOT let unstarted phases accumulate across iters: a phase
48	    with no blueprint is a phase that cannot be parallelised, cannot
49	    be reviewed, and cannot be handed to a prover. Writing the
50	    blueprint early is the cheapest action in the loop.
51	
52	  ### HARD GATE — per-file prover dispatch
53	
54	  This is the rule that protects the project from low-quality prover work.
55	  Apply it verbatim, every iter, no exceptions:
56	
57	  - For each `.lean` file F you are considering adding to
58	    `## Current Objectives` (i.e. about to send a prover to), identify
59	    the corresponding blueprint chapter C. The mapping is: if some
60	    chapter declares `% archon:covers ... F ...` near its top, THAT
61	    chapter is C (a consolidated chapter blueprints several files while
62	    its siblings are thin pointers); otherwise fall back to the 1:1
63	    `Foo/Bar.lean → Foo_Bar.tex` slug. Look up C in my per-chapter
64	    checklist. When a chapter declares `covers:`, its single verdict
65	    gates EVERY file it lists — so a `correct: false` on the
66	    consolidated chapter defers all of them, and one writer directive
67	    against that chapter fixes the content behind all of them.
68	  - If C has `complete: true` AND `correct: true` AND no must-fix-this-iter
69	    finding touches it, F may go into the objectives.
70	  - Otherwise (C is `partial | false` on either axis, OR a must-fix
71	    finding names C, OR a broken `\uses{}` in C points at a label F's
72	    blueprint depends on):
73	    1. DROP F from this iter's objectives (by default — but see the
74	       same-iter fast path below).
75	    2. Dispatch the catalog's blueprint-writing subagent for C THIS
76	       iter with a directive targeting the specific must-fix items I
77	       flagged.
78	    3. Record in iter/iter-NNN/plan.md why F was at risk and what the
79	       writer was asked to fix.
80	  - **Same-iter fast path (sanctioned — removes a wasted iter).** After
81	    the writer returns AND `lake build` is green, you MAY re-dispatch me
82	    scoped to C alone — a fresh slug, a directive naming only C and the
83	    must-fix items I flagged. If that scoped re-review returns C as
84	    `complete: true` AND `correct: true` with no must-fix finding, the
85	    gate is satisfied for C: add F to THIS iter's objectives and send a
86	    prover. No need to burn an iter waiting. If the scoped re-review
87	    still fails, F stays deferred.
88	  - If you do NOT take the fast path, F simply waits: the next iter's
89	    mandatory dispatch of me re-confirms C before any prover runs on F.
90	    The fast path is purely a latency optimization — it never weakens
91	    the gate. F enters objectives ONLY on a fresh `complete + correct`
92	    verdict with no must-fix, whether that verdict arrives this iter (via
93	    the fast path) or next (via the mandatory dispatch). A green build
94	    alone is NOT sufficient; the re-review is what clears the gate.
95	
96	  ### Strategy / multi-route handling
97	
98	  - If the strategy has multiple viable routes and I report that one
99	    or more routes have no blueprint coverage, dispatch the catalog's
100	    blueprint-writing subagent (one call per missing-coverage route)
101	    in the same iteration. Do not let provers begin work on a route
102	    until its blueprint coverage is in place.
103	  - If I flag a definition that may require a strategy modification,
104	    treat that as a STRATEGY.md update task before any further Lean work
105	    — provers cannot be dispatched until the strategy update is reflected
106	    in the blueprint.
107	
108	  ### What "deferred" means in practice
109	
110	  Deferring a prover round is the correct, intentional action — not a
111	  failure. The 1-iter latency cost of waiting for a writer is small
112	  compared to the cost of a prover formalizing a broken blueprint and
113	  the work being thrown away. Log the deferral cleanly in plan.md and
114	  move on; the next iter's mandatory me-dispatch will green-light F.
115	---
116	
117	# Blueprint Reviewer
118	
119	You read the **entire blueprint** plus a context bundle from the plan agent and produce a per-chapter checklist + a set of cross-cutting summaries. You are **read-only on project source** and the blueprint — your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
120	
121	You are **mandatory in the plan phase**: the plan agent dispatches you every iteration before writing prover objectives. Your output is the plan agent's primary window into blueprint health, so the plan agent doesn't have to read every chapter itself.
122	
123	## Your Job
124	
125	The plan agent gives you a directive containing the current strategy snapshot, the references you should treat as authoritative, and any specific concerns. You then audit:
126	
127	- **Completeness** — for each chapter, are the definitions / theorems the strategy says the project needs actually present? Is the proof sketch detailed enough for a prover to formalize without guessing? Are the cross-references (`\uses{...}`) accurate?
128	- **Correctness** — does any definition contradict its references? Does any proof sketch contain a step that doesn't follow? Does any `\lean{...}` hint name a declaration that doesn't exist or has the wrong signature?
129	- **Lean target formulation quality** — for each `\lean{...}` hint, is the named theorem/definition a *useful* target for the prover? Vague or under-specified hints lead to wrong formalizations; surface those.
130	- **Multi-route coverage** — if the strategy lists multiple viable routes (alternative proof approaches, alternative definitions), is each route represented in the blueprint? Routes the strategy mentions but the blueprint does not cover are red flags.
131	- **Unstarted phases** — for each phase in the strategy that has no blueprint chapter at all, produce a concrete chapter outline (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" below). This is a proactive planning function, not just a gap report.
132	- **Citation discipline** — for every definition / theorem / lemma block that derives from external reference material, audit all four elements:
133	  1. **`% SOURCE:` pointer with local-file parenthetical.** Format must be `% SOURCE: <pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. Verify the named local file EXISTS under `references/`. A `% SOURCE:` with no `(read from …)` parenthetical, or with a parenthetical naming a file that doesn't exist on disk, is a hard fail — the writer fabricated the citation.
134	  2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` verbatim text** for definitions / theorems / lemmas. Audit dimensions:
135	     - **Original language**: the quote must be in the source's original language. A quote in English when the source is Bourbaki / EGA (French) signals translation, which is not allowed — flag it.
136	     - **Original notation**: the quote must use the source's notation, even when it differs from the project's. If the project writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ everywhere but the `% SOURCE QUOTE:` also writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ when the source is Hartshorne (who writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$), the quote was rewritten — flag it.
137	     - **Verbatim, every word**: a quote that reads like a paraphrase ("essentially says that …", "the source states …") rather than direct copy is a hard fail. The whole point of the verbatim is anti-hallucination; paraphrased quotes are exactly the failure mode.
138	  3. **`% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`** immediately before the `\begin{proof}` environment, when the block has a proof and the proof derives from the source. Same verbatim rules as `% SOURCE QUOTE:`. Missing `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` on a theorem whose proof clearly comes from the cited source is a citation-discipline finding. (Archon-original proofs of external statements are allowed — flag only when the proof prose itself reads as a translation of an obvious source proof.)
139	  4. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}`** line as the first line of the block's prose. Missing → flag.
140	
141	  Cross-check: the visible `\textit{Source: ...}` pointer must match the `% SOURCE:` pointer. Drift between them signals copy-paste error or hallucination.
142	
143	  Spot-check against `## References consulted` in the corresponding writer's report (when available in `task_results/`): every distinct `references/<file>.md` named in `% SOURCE:` parentheticals across the chapter should appear in that list. A `% SOURCE: ... (read from references/X.md)` where the writer's "References consulted" list does NOT mention `references/X.md` means the writer cited a file they did not actually open this session — fabrication.
144	
145	  **Archon-original / project-bespoke** results (no external source) omit the source lines entirely — do not falsely flag those. The signal that a block is Archon-original: the directive that produced it didn't name an external source, or the chapter prose explicitly characterizes it as new (e.g. "This is the technical heart of our argument"). When in doubt, ask the plan agent in "Notes for Plan Agent" rather than flagging.
146	
147	You audit the blueprint **against the context the plan agent gave you**, not against your own opinions about how the math should be set up. But you are critical of weak prose — under-specified blueprints fail provers and are not safe to merge.
148	
149	`/references/summary.md` lists the project's reference materials. The planner writes the blueprints, while its knowledge might be enough to write some parts of the blueprint, some parts may be subject to hallucination and require reference material. If you believe a reference is required to write mathematically correct and complete blueprint chapters, you should mention it in your report so that the planner can retrieve it for the writer.
150	
151	## Always read everything
152	
153	**Read every chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/`**, no exceptions, regardless of project size. The cross-chapter view is the entire reason for running me. If the directive contains a "scope" hint, treat it as a focus suggestion (which chapters need extra attention), not as a permission to skip reading.
154	
155	## Unstarted-phase proposals
156	
157	This is a proactive function — you produce it in addition to the per-chapter audit, not instead of it.
158	
159	After completing the per-chapter checklist, cross-reference the strategy snapshot's `## Phases & estimations` table against the set of chapters you just read. For each phase row whose `Status` is not `complete` and whose content has **zero blueprint coverage** (no chapter exists, or the only existing chapter is a stub with fewer than ~3 meaningful declaration blocks), produce a **chapter outline proposal**.
160	
161	A chapter outline proposal is not a flag or a complaint — it is a concrete, actionable seed that the plan agent can hand directly to a blueprint-writing subagent. It must contain enough mathematical detail that a writer receiving it as a directive can produce a complete chapter without further research by the plan agent.
162	
163	The proposal answers:
164	
165	1. **Which Lean file(s) will this chapter cover?** Name the expected file path(s) and whether this should be a consolidated chapter (one chapter covering multiple files via `% archon:covers`) or a 1:1 chapter.
166	
167	2. **What are the declaration blocks the chapter needs?** For each: the block type (definition / lemma / theorem / proposition), a proposed `\label`, a one-sentence description of the mathematical content, the expected `\lean{...}` hint (even if speculative — tag it `[expected]`), and the likely reference source (from `references/summary.md` or a named standard reference). Do not write the full prose — that is the writer's job — but give enough that the writer knows exactly what to write.
168	
169	3. **What are the internal dependencies?** Which declarations within this chapter use others (the `\uses{...}` graph in skeletal form). Identifying this upfront prevents the writer from writing declarations in an order that creates circular `\uses` references.
170	
171	4. **What is the proof strategy for the chapter's main theorem(s)?** Two to four sentences naming the key steps, the supporting lemmas, and any Mathlib infrastructure the proofs will depend on. This is the piece most likely to require a reference; if you can identify the relevant section of a source in `references/summary.md`, name it.
172	
173	5. **What references should the blueprint-writer read first?** List the specific local files under `references/` that are most relevant, and the sections within them. If no local file covers the material, flag it as a retrieval need so the plan agent can dispatch a reference-retriever before the writer.
174	
175	6. **Are there subphase choices the outline exposes?** If the chapter's material admits multiple natural decompositions (e.g. the main theorem could be approached via method A or method B, or the definition could be bundled or unbundled), name them explicitly. The plan agent cannot make an informed subphase choice without seeing what the choices are — surfacing them here is the primary value of writing the blueprint early rather than late.
176	
177	**Format for each proposal** (one block per unstarted phase):
178	
179	```
180	### Proposed chapter: <expected filename, e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Foo_Bar.tex`>
181	
182	**Covers**: `Foo/Bar.lean` (+ any sibling files if consolidated)
183	**Strategy phase**: <phase name from STRATEGY.md>
184	**Why now**: <one sentence on why writing this chapter this iter, before any prover work, is the right move — what ambiguity it resolves, what parallelism it enables>
185	
186	**Key declarations** (in dependency order):
187	1. `\definition` `\label{def:foo}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.foo}` [expected]. Source: <reference + section, or "no source identified — Archon-original">
188	2. `\lemma` `\label{lem:bar}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.bar}` [expected]. Source: <...>
189	3. `\theorem` `\label{thm:baz}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.baz}` [expected]. Source: <...>
190	...
191	
192	**`\uses` skeleton**:
193	- `thm:baz` uses `lem:bar`, `def:foo`
194	- `lem:bar` uses `def:foo`
195	
196	**Main theorem proof strategy**: <2–4 sentences>
197	
198	**References for writer**:
199	- `references/<slug>.md` → `<file>.<ext>`, §<section> — <why relevant>
200	- <or: "retrieval needed: <source name> — no local file exists yet">
201	
202	**Subphase choices exposed**:
203	- Choice A vs Choice B: <one sentence describing each option and the trade-off>. Recommendation: <your read on which is better given the strategy, or "unclear — plan agent should decide">
204	- (omit this field if the chapter has a clear single decomposition)
205	```
206	
207	**Tone and completeness.** The proposal should be aggressive about detail — it is cheaper to write a thorough proposal now than to have the writer ask for clarification later. If the phase is large enough that one chapter cannot cover it, say so and propose the split (two or more chapter outlines, each covering a natural sub-unit).
208	
209	**Do not propose chapters for phases that already have adequate coverage.** A phase with ≥3 meaningful declaration blocks across its chapters is covered; do not reproduce a proposal for it. The signal for "adequate" is the same as for the per-chapter checklist: `complete: true` (or `partial` with only minor gaps) on the phase's relevant chapters.
210	
211	**Proposals are must-act-this-iter.** They land in `## Must-fix-this-iter` under the label `unstarted-phase proposal` alongside the hard-gate findings. The plan agent must either dispatch a blueprint-writer for each proposed chapter or record an explicit one-line deferral rationale in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`. Silently ignoring the proposals is the same failure mode as silently ignoring a CHURNING verdict.
212	
213	## Directive Format
214	
215	```markdown
216	# Blueprint Reviewer Directive
217	
218	## Slug
219	<slug>
220	
221	## Strategy snapshot
222	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted: the project's end-state and the chapters that bear on it. Tells you what each chapter MUST contain to support the strategy. MUST include the full `## Phases & estimations` table so you can identify unstarted phases.>
223	
224	## Routes
225	<if the strategy has more than one viable route, list each route here with one line on what's distinctive about it and which chapters / definitions are exclusive to that route. If only one route, write "single route".>
226	
227	## References
228	- <path/to/reference.md>: <topic + which chapters depend on it>
229	- <arxiv ID>: <topic>
230	
231	## Focus areas (optional)
232	<chapters or theorems the plan agent wants extra attention on this iter — bias for thoroughness here, do not skip the others>
233	
234	## Known issues
235	<things the plan agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
236	```
237	
238	## What you do
239	
240	1. **Read your directive completely.** Extract the `## Phases & estimations` table — you will need it for the unstarted-phase proposals.
241	2. **List every chapter** under `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`.
242	3. **For each chapter** (no exceptions, no scope shortcuts):
243	   - Read the entire chapter.
244	   - Check every declaration block against the strategy snapshot.
245	   - For each proof block: are steps sound? Are `\uses{...}` cross-refs real labels? Is detail adequate for a prover?
246	   - For each `\lean{...}`: is the named target well-formulated? (You may verify existence using the `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools — read-only.)
247	4. **Compute completeness/correctness verdicts** per chapter (`true | partial | false`).
248	5. **Note cross-chapter inconsistencies** as you find them (e.g. `def X` in chapter A doesn't match the use of `X` in chapter B). These go in the "Cross-chapter notes" section.
249	6. **Check multi-route coverage**: for each route listed in the directive's `## Routes`, identify which chapters cover it. Flag any route that has zero or insufficient blueprint coverage.
250	7. **Cross-reference phases against chapters**: for each row in the strategy's `## Phases & estimations` table, determine whether adequate blueprint coverage exists. For every phase with no or stub coverage, produce a chapter outline proposal (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" above).
251	8. **Produce three top-level summaries** (see report format) — these are what the plan agent acts on first.
252	
253	You may also use:
254	- `archon-lean-lsp`: read-only Lean LSP operations (search, hover, diagnostics) to verify `\lean{...}` references.
255	- `${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/dependency_graph.py" .` — quick view of the project's Lean ↔ blueprint dependency map.
256	
257	You do **not** modify any project file, including the blueprint. Even if you spot a clear fix, you report it; the plan agent decides what to do next iter.
258	
259	## Report format
260	
261	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-reviewer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
262	
263	**Omit-empty rule.** Required sections: `## Slug`, `## Iteration`, `## Per-chapter` (the HARD GATE depends on it), `## Severity summary`, `Overall verdict`. **Everything else is optional and must be omitted when empty.**
264	
265	Concretely:
266	
267	- `### Incomplete parts`, `### Proofs lacking detail`, `### Lean difficulty quality`, `### Multi-route coverage`, `### Citation discipline` under `## Top-level summaries`: omit each sub-section whose finding list is empty. If all five are empty, omit `## Top-level summaries` entirely — the per-chapter table already encodes "everything is fine".
268	- `## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals`: omit only when every phase in the strategy has adequate blueprint coverage. When present, this section is never empty — each proposal block is a concrete outline, not a one-line flag.
269	- `## Cross-chapter notes`: omit when no cross-chapter findings exist (the most common case on a clean blueprint).

274	Filling templates with hollow "(none)" / "no drift" / "OK" content per chapter is the bloat the report should not produce. A clean 11-chapter audit should be a few hundred lines, not 16K tokens.

3	\chapter{Weil divisors on a smooth proper curve (RR.1)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II \S6, pp.~130--137 +
7	% IV.1, pp.~294--296 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
8	% Stacks pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (divisors),
9	% 02ME (order at a point on a regular 1-dimensional scheme),
10	% 0BE0 (degree of a divisor on a curve), 0BE3 (principal divisors have
11	% degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve).
12	
13	\section{Setup and motivation}
14	
15	This chapter is the first of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
16	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
17	\[
18	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
19	  \,\colon\,
20	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with
21	  $g(C) = 0$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.}
22	\]
23	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
24	divisor of a closed point on a curve (\texttt{RR.1}, this chapter); the
25	Riemann--Roch dimension formula $\ell(D) = \deg D + 1$ for $\deg D \geq 0$ on
26	a genus-$0$ curve (\texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections argument identifying
27	$\dim_k H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ via $1$ and a non-constant function
28	(\texttt{RR.3}); the ``rational curve $\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1$''
29	classification recovered as a degree-$1$ morphism into $\mathbb P^1$ via
30	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
31	
32	Mathlib ships no \texttt{WeilDivisor} on a scheme. Adjacent pieces exist, but
33	none of them is the data structure we need: \texttt{MeromorphicOn.divisor}
34	(\texttt{Mathlib.Analysis.Meromorphic.Divisor}) is the order function of a
35	meromorphic function valued in a \emph{normed} field, not a formal sum of
36	codimension-$1$ cycles on a scheme; \texttt{CommRing.Pic}
37	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) is the ring-level Picard group of
38	invertible modules under tensor product, with no scheme-level analogue;
39	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap}
40	(\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}) packages rational
41	maps but not divisors. Consequently this chapter, and the three siblings, are
42	\emph{project-bespoke}: the only Mathlib tools they rely on are
43	discrete-valuation rings, the function field, and the ambient scheme API.
44	The audit grounding this scope decision is in
45	\texttt{analogies/rrbridge-survey.md}.
46	
47	This chapter establishes the formal-sum data type
48	$\Div(X) = \bigoplus_{\substack{Z \subset X \\ \codim 1}} \mathbb Z$ on a
49	Noetherian integral scheme $X$ satisfying Hartshorne's condition $(*)$, the
50	principal-divisor homomorphism $\div \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \Div(X)$ on a
51	curve, the degree map $\deg \colon \Div(C) \to \mathbb Z$ on a smooth proper
52	curve over an algebraically closed field, and the linear-equivalence
53	relation. The \emph{degree-zero of a principal divisor} statement
54	(\Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero}) is included for completeness; its proof
55	decomposes into a separate sub-build inside \texttt{RR.1} (it uses the
56	auxiliary lemma that any non-constant rational function on $C$ defines a
57	finite morphism $C \to \mathbb P^1$). The line-bundle construction $\mathcal
58	O_C(D)$ and the associated cohomology computation $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))$
59	live in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3}; the Riemann--Roch dimension
60	formula lives in \texttt{RR.2}; the genus-$0$ classification proper lives in
61	\texttt{RR.4}.
62	
63	Throughout this chapter, the standing convention is Hartshorne's condition
64	$(*)$:
65	
66	% SOURCE QUOTE: "$(\ast)$ $X$ is a noetherian integral separated scheme which
67	% is regular in codimension one."
68	\textit{Source: Hartshorne, II \S6, p.~130, the conditional $(*)$.}
69	\[
70	  (\ast)\qquad
71	  X \text{ is a noetherian integral separated scheme which is regular in
72	  codimension one.}
73	\]
74	For the degree map and for principal-divisor statements on a curve, we
75	additionally assume $X$ is a complete (i.e.\ proper) nonsingular curve over
76	an algebraically closed field $\bar k$, where Hartshorne's condition $(*)$ is
77	automatic.
78	
79	\section{Codim-$1$ cycle group}
80	
81	\begin{definition}
82	[Codim-$1$ cycle group / Weil divisor group of a scheme]
83	  \label{def:codim1_cycles}
84	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor}
85	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "prime divisor" and
86	  % "Weil divisor" under condition $(*)$) (read from
87	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 147).
88	  % Stacks pointer: tag 02RW.
89	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $X$ satisfy $(\ast)$. A \emph{prime
90	  % divisor} on $X$ is a closed integral subscheme $Y$ of codimension one.
91	  % A \emph{Weil divisor} is an element of the free abelian group
92	  % $\operatorname{Div} X$ generated by the prime divisors. We write a
93	  % divisor as $D = \sum n_i Y_i$, where the $Y_i$ are prime divisors, the
94	  % $n_i$ are integers, and only finitely many $n_i$ are different from
95	  % zero. If all the $n_i \geq 0$, we say that $D$ is \emph{effective}."
96	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130.}
97	  Let $X$ be a Noetherian integral separated scheme that is regular in
98	  codimension one (i.e.\ $X$ satisfies $(*)$). A \emph{prime divisor} on
99	  $X$ is a closed integral subscheme $Y \subset X$ of codimension one. The
100	  \emph{Weil divisor group}
101	  \[
102	    \Div(X) \;=\; \bigoplus_{\substack{Y \subset X \\ \text{prime divisor}}}
103	    \mathbb Z \cdot Y
104	  \]
105	  is the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of $X$; an element
106	  $D = \sum n_i Y_i$ with finitely many nonzero coefficients $n_i \in
107	  \mathbb Z$ is called a \emph{Weil divisor}, and is \emph{effective} when
108	  all $n_i \geq 0$.
109	\end{definition}
110	
111	\section{Order of a rational function at a prime divisor}
112	
113	\begin{definition}
114	[Order of a rational function at a prime divisor]
115	  \label{def:order_at_point}
116	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order}
117	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
118	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130--131 (valuation $v_Y$ of a prime
119	  % divisor; "zero/pole of $f$ along $Y$") (read from
120	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
121	  % Stacks pointer: tag 02ME.
122	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If $Y$ is a prime divisor on $X$, let $\eta \in Y$ be its
123	  % generic point. Then the local ring $\mathcal O_{\eta, X}$ of $X$ at
124	  % $\eta$ is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field $K$, the
125	  % function field of $X$. We call the corresponding discrete valuation
126	  % $v_Y$ the \emph{valuation of $Y$}."
127	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let $f \in K^\ast$ be any nonzero rational function
128	  % on $X$. Then $v_Y(f)$ is an integer. If it is positive, we say $f$ has
129	  % a \emph{zero} along $Y$, of that order; if it is negative, we say $f$
130	  % has a \emph{pole} along $Y$, of order $-v_Y(f)$."
131	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation $v_Y$).}
132	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. For a prime divisor $Y \subset X$ with generic
133	  point $\eta \in Y$, the local ring $\mathcal O_{X, \eta}$ is a discrete
134	  valuation ring (by regularity in codimension one) with quotient field the
135	  function field $K(X)$. For a nonzero rational function $f \in K(X)^{\times}$
136	  the \emph{order of $f$ along $Y$} is the integer
137	  \[
138	    \ord_Y(f) \;:=\; v_Y(f) \;\in\; \mathbb Z,
139	  \]
140	  where $v_Y$ is the normalised discrete valuation associated to
141	  $\mathcal O_{X, \eta}$. If $\ord_Y(f) > 0$, $f$ has a \emph{zero of
142	  order $\ord_Y(f)$ along $Y$}; if $\ord_Y(f) < 0$, $f$ has a \emph{pole
143	  of order $-\ord_Y(f)$ along $Y$}.
144	
145	  Specialisation to a curve. If $C$ is a smooth proper curve over $\bar k$
146	  and $P \in C$ is a closed point, then $P$ is automatically a prime
147	  divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the $1$-dimensional
148	  scheme $C$); the local ring $\mathcal O_{C, P}$ is a discrete valuation
149	  ring by smoothness, and $\ord_P(f) = v_P(f)$ is the standard DVR
150	  valuation of $f$ at $P$.
151	\end{definition}
152	
153	\section{Divisor of a closed point on a curve}
154	
155	\begin{definition}
156	[Divisor of a closed point on a curve]
157	  \label{def:divisor_closed_point}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint}
159	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
160	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
161	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154):
162	  % "A prime divisor is just a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can
163	  % be written $D = \sum n_i P_i$, where the $P_i$ are closed points, and
164	  % $n_i \in \mathbf Z$."
165	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now we come to the study of divisors on curves. If $X$
166	  % is a nonsingular curve, then $X$ satisfies the condition $(\ast)$ used
167	  % above, so we can talk about divisors on $X$. A prime divisor is just
168	  % a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can be written $D = \sum n_i
169	  % P_i$, where the $P_i$ are closed points, and $n_i \in \mathbf Z$."
170	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (``Divisors on Curves'').}
171	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over a field $k$ and let $P \in C$ be a
172	  closed point. The \emph{Weil divisor associated to $P$} is the element
173	  \[
174	    [P] \;:=\; 1 \cdot P \;\in\; \Div(C)
175	  \]
176	  i.e.\ the prime divisor $P$ with multiplicity one. The assignment is
177	  well-defined because the closed point $P$ is automatically a codimension-$1$
178	  integral closed subscheme of the $1$-dimensional integral scheme $C$.
179	  Conversely, every prime divisor on $C$ is of this form, so an arbitrary
180	  divisor on $C$ is a finite formal $\mathbb Z$-linear combination
181	  $\sum n_i [P_i]$ of closed points.
182	\end{definition}
183	
184	\section{Degree of a divisor over an algebraically closed base}
185	
186	\begin{definition}
187	[Degree of a divisor on a curve over $\bar k$]
188	  \label{def:divisor_degree}
189	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree}
190	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:divisor_closed_point}
191	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
192	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154).
193	  % Stacks pointer: tag 0BE0.
194	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We define the \emph{degree} of $D$ to be $\sum n_i$."
195	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (definition of degree on a
196	  nonsingular curve).}
197	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
198	  $\bar k$. The \emph{degree} of a Weil divisor $D = \sum n_i [P_i] \in
199	  \Div(C)$ is the integer
200	  \[
201	    \deg(D) \;:=\; \sum_i n_i \;\in\; \mathbb Z.
202	  \]
203	  The definition uses that every closed point of $C$ has residue field
204	  $\bar k$ (so each prime divisor contributes degree one); over a general
205	  field $k$ the same definition with the residue-field-degree weight
206	  $\deg(D) := \sum_i n_i \, [\kappa(P_i) : k]$ recovers the
207	  \emph{geometric} degree, but the project's RR bridge needs only the
208	  $\bar k$ specialisation.
209	\end{definition}
210	
211	\begin{theorem}
212	[The degree map is a group homomorphism]
213	  \label{thm:divisor_degree_hom}
214	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_hom}
215	  \uses{def:divisor_degree}
216	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
217	  $\bar k$. The degree map of \Cref{def:divisor_degree} is a group
218	  homomorphism
219	  \[
220	    \deg \colon \Div(C) \longrightarrow \mathbb Z,
221	    \qquad
222	    \deg(D_1 + D_2) \;=\; \deg(D_1) + \deg(D_2),
223	    \qquad
224	    \deg(-D) \;=\; -\deg(D).
225	  \]
226	\end{theorem}
227	
228	\begin{proof}
229	  \uses{def:divisor_degree}
230	  Immediate from the definition: $\Div(C)$ is the free abelian group on the
231	  set of closed points of $C$, and $\deg$ is the unique group homomorphism
232	  sending each generator $[P]$ to $1 \in \mathbb Z$ (equivalently: the sum
233	  of the multiplicities). Both additivity and negation thus reduce to the
234	  corresponding identities for finite sums of integers.
235	\end{proof}
236	
237	\section{Principal divisors}
238	
239	\begin{definition}
240	[Principal divisor of a rational function]
241	  \label{def:principal_divisor}
242	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal}
243	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:order_at_point}
244	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Lemma~6.1 + the definition of $\operatorname{div}(f) = (f)$,
245	  % p.~131 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
246	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Lemma 6.1. Let $X$ satisfy $(\ast)$, and let $f \in K^\ast$
247	  % be a nonzero function on $X$. Then $v_Y(f) = 0$ for all except finitely
248	  % many prime divisors $Y$."
249	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $X$ satisfy $(\ast)$ and let $f \in K^\ast$.
250	  % We define the \emph{divisor of $f$}, denoted $(f)$, by
251	  % $(f) = \sum v_Y(f) \cdot Y$, where the sum is taken over all prime
252	  % divisors $Y$ of $X$. By the lemma, this is a finite sum, hence is a
253	  % divisor. Any divisor which is equal to the divisor of a function is
254	  % called a \emph{principal divisor}."
255	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Lemma~6.1 and the following definition,
256	  p.~131.}
257	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. For a nonzero rational function $f \in K(X)^{\times}$,
258	  Hartshorne's Lemma~6.1 asserts that $\ord_Y(f) = 0$ for all but finitely
259	  many prime divisors $Y$ on $X$; hence the formal sum
260	  \[
261	    \div(f) \;:=\; \sum_{\substack{Y \subset X \\ \text{prime divisor}}}
262	    \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y \;\in\; \Div(X)
263	  \]
264	  has finite support and is a well-defined Weil divisor, called the
265	  \emph{principal divisor of $f$}. On a smooth proper curve $C$ over $\bar k$
266	  the same definition specialises to
267	  $\div(f) = \sum_{P \in C \text{ closed}} \ord_P(f) \cdot [P]$, the formal
268	  sum over closed points.
269	\end{definition}
270	
271	\begin{theorem}
272	[The principal-divisor map is a group homomorphism]
273	  \label{thm:principal_hom}
274	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_hom}
275	  \uses{def:principal_divisor, def:order_at_point}
276	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, the paragraph after the principal-divisor
277	  % definition, p.~131 (read from
278	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
279	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Note that if $f, g \in K^\ast$, then $(f/g) = (f) - (g)$
280	  % because of the properties of valuations. Therefore sending a function
281	  % $f$ to its divisor $(f)$ gives a homomorphism of the multiplicative
282	  % group $K^\ast$ to the additive group $\operatorname{Div} X$, and the
283	  % image, which consists of the principal divisors, is a subgroup of
284	  % $\operatorname{Div} X$."
285	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~131 (group-homomorphism remark).}
286	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. The principal-divisor map
287	  $\div \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \Div(X)$ is a group homomorphism from the
288	  multiplicative group of nonzero rational functions to the additive group
289	  of Weil divisors. Concretely
290	  \[
291	    \div(fg) \;=\; \div(f) + \div(g),
292	    \qquad
293	    \div(f^{-1}) \;=\; -\div(f),
294	    \qquad
295	    \div(1) \;=\; 0.
296	  \]
297	\end{theorem}
298	
299	\begin{proof}
300	  \uses{def:principal_divisor, def:order_at_point}
301	  Each prime divisor $Y$ on $X$ contributes a discrete valuation $v_Y$ on
302	  $K(X)$ (\Cref{def:order_at_point}); by the standard DVR axioms,
303	  $v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)$ and $v_Y(f^{-1}) = -v_Y(f)$ for $f, g \in
304	  K(X)^{\times}$, and $v_Y(1) = 0$. Summing these identities over all prime
305	  divisors of $X$ (a sum that has finite support on each factor by
306	  \Cref{def:principal_divisor}, so the support of the sum is the union
307	  of the two finite supports and remains finite) gives the three displayed
308	  identities.
309	\end{proof}
310	
311	\begin{theorem}
312	[Principal divisors have degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve]
313	  \label{thm:principal_deg_zero}
314	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_degree_zero}
315	  \uses{def:divisor_degree, def:principal_divisor, thm:principal_hom}
316	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Corollary~6.10, p.~138 (read from
317	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 155).
318	  % Stacks pointer: tag 0BE3.
319	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Corollary 6.10. \emph{A principal divisor on a complete
320	  % nonsingular curve $X$ has degree zero. Consequently the degree function
321	  % induces a surjective homomorphism $\deg \colon \operatorname{Cl} X \to
322	  % \mathbf Z$.}"
323	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Corollary~6.10, p.~138.}
324	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
325	  $\bar k$. For every nonzero rational function $f \in K(C)^{\times}$ the
326	  associated principal divisor has degree zero:
327	  \[
328	    \deg(\div(f)) \;=\; 0 \;\in\; \mathbb Z.
329	  \]
330	\end{theorem}
331	
332	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Let $f \in K(X)^\ast$. If $f \in k$, then
333	% $(f) = 0$, so there is nothing to prove. If $f \notin k$, then the
334	% inclusion of fields $k(f) \subset K(X)$ induces a finite morphism
335	% $\varphi \colon X \to \mathbf P^1$. It is a morphism by (I, 6.12). We
336	% have $(f) = \varphi^\ast(\{0\} - \{\infty\})$. Since $\{0\} - \{\infty\}$
337	% is a divisor of degree 0 on $\mathbf P^1$, we conclude that $(f)$ has
338	% degree 0 on $X$."
339	\begin{proof}
340	  \uses{def:divisor_degree, def:principal_divisor, thm:principal_hom}
341	  Following Hartshorne's Corollary~6.10. If $f \in \bar k \subset K(C)$ is
342	  a constant scalar, then $f$ has no zeros or poles, so $\div(f) = 0$ and
343	  $\deg(\div(f)) = 0$. Otherwise the inclusion of fields
344	  $\bar k(f) \subset K(C)$ exhibits $K(C)$ as a finite extension of the
345	  rational function field $\bar k(f) \cong \bar k(t)$, so the corresponding
346	  morphism $\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ is finite (by the
347	  smooth-proper-curve / function-field correspondence on which a curve is
348	  determined by its function field, classical I.6.12). The principal
349	  divisor $\div(f)$ equals the pullback $\varphi^{*}([0] - [\infty])$
350	  along $\varphi$ of the degree-$0$ divisor $[0] - [\infty]$ on
351	  $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$; since pullback along a finite morphism
352	  multiplies degrees by $\deg(\varphi)$, we conclude
353	  $\deg(\div(f)) = \deg(\varphi) \cdot \deg([0] - [\infty]) = \deg(\varphi)
354	  \cdot 0 = 0$.
355	
356	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The two auxiliary statements ``the inclusion
357	  $\bar k(f) \subset K(C)$ defines a finite morphism $C \to \mathbb P^1$''
358	  and ``pullback along a finite morphism of curves multiplies degrees by
359	  the degree of the morphism'' (Hartshorne's Proposition~II.6.9, the
360	  multiplicativity of degree) are themselves separable sub-lemmas of
361	  \texttt{RR.1}; they are stated here for completeness and slotted to be
362	  closed in a follow-up iteration. The signature
363	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal\_degree\_zero}
364	  is recorded as the public face of this theorem and is depended on by
365	  the \texttt{RR.3}/\texttt{RR.4} chain.
366	\end{proof}
367	
368	\section{Divisor class group}
369	
370	We follow Hartshorne II.6 (pp.~131--132) and define the divisor class group
371	of a curve as the cokernel of the principal-divisor homomorphism. This is
372	the \emph{Picard group} of $C$ in the curve-specific sense; the line-bundle
373	interpretation $\Cl(C) \cong \Pic(C)$ requires the construction of
374	$\mathcal O_C(D)$, which lives in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3} and is
375	out of scope here.
376	
377	\begin{definition}
378	[Linear equivalence of divisors]
379	  \label{def:linear_equivalence}
380	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.LinearEquivalence}
381	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:principal_divisor, thm:principal_hom}
382	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~131 (definition of linear equivalence
383	  % and of $\operatorname{Cl} X$) (read from
384	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
385	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $X$ satisfy $(\ast)$. Two divisors $D$
386	  % and $D'$ are said to be \emph{linearly equivalent}, written $D \sim D'$,
387	  % if $D - D'$ is a principal divisor. The group $\operatorname{Div} X$ of
388	  % all divisors divided by the subgroup of principal divisors is called
389	  % the \emph{divisor class group} of $X$, and is denoted by
390	  % $\operatorname{Cl} X$."
391	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~131 (linear equivalence and
392	  $\operatorname{Cl} X$).}
393	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. Two Weil divisors $D, D' \in \Div(X)$ are
394	  \emph{linearly equivalent}, written $D \sim D'$, if and only if there
395	  exists $f \in K(X)^{\times}$ with
396	  \[
397	    D - D' \;=\; \div(f) \;\in\; \Div(X).
398	  \]
399	  The relation $\sim$ is an equivalence relation on $\Div(X)$ (the
400	  reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity all transport from the analogous
401	  identities $\div(1) = 0$, $\div(f^{-1}) = -\div(f)$, $\div(fg) =
402	  \div(f) + \div(g)$ via \Cref{thm:principal_hom}); the corresponding
403	  quotient group $\Cl(X) := \Div(X) / \mathrm{im}(\div)$ is the
404	  \emph{divisor class group} of $X$. On a smooth proper curve $C$ over
405	  $\bar k$, \Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero} implies that the degree map
406	  $\deg \colon \Div(C) \to \mathbb Z$ descends to a homomorphism
407	  $\deg \colon \Cl(C) \to \mathbb Z$.
408	\end{definition}
409	
410	\section{Out of scope}
411	
412	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.1} and
413	are sequenced into the sibling sub-build chapters:
414	
415	\begin{itemize}
416	  \item The line-bundle construction $\mathcal O_C(D)$ associated to a Weil
417	    divisor $D$, the calculation of its global sections, and the
418	    identification $\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')$ when
419	    $D \sim D'$, lives in \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
420	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added).
421	  \item The Riemann--Roch dimension formula
422	    $\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g$ and its genus-$0$ specialisation
423	    $\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1$ for $\deg(D) \geq 0$ live in \texttt{RR.2}
424	    (sibling chapter \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RRFormula.tex}, to be added).
425	  \item The classification ``a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
426	    curve over $\bar k$ with $g = 0$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$''
427	    (Hartshorne Example~IV.1.3.5) is the headline of \texttt{RR.4}
428	    (sibling chapter \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalIsoP1.tex}, to be added)
429	    and assembles \texttt{RR.1}/\texttt{RR.2}/\texttt{RR.3} into the
430	    \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} morphism into $\mathbb P^1$ together
431	    with its degree-$1$ identification.
432	  \item \emph{Cartier divisors} (locally principal divisors on a possibly
433	    singular scheme) are out of scope: the project's curves are smooth, so
434	    Weil and Cartier divisors coincide, and the simpler Weil form suffices.
435	    A future Mathlib upstream PR could introduce the Cartier formalism.
436	  \item \emph{Higher-codimension cycles} (the Chow group
437	    $\bigoplus_i \mathrm{CH}^i(X)$) are out of scope: this chapter is
438	    strictly curve-focused.
439	  \item The proof of \Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero} factors through two
440	    auxiliary statements (``finite morphism induced by a non-constant
441	    rational function'' and ``multiplicativity of degree under finite
442	    pullback'', Hartshorne II.6.9) which are themselves separable
443	    sub-lemmas of \texttt{RR.1}; they are flagged in the proof body but
444	    deliberately not unfolded as standalone declarations here.
445	\end{itemize}

1	\chapter{Abelian-variety rigidity: morphisms from a genus-$0$ curve into an abelian variety are constant}
2	\label{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects.lean AlgebraicJacobian/RigidityLemma.lean
4	
5	% Local macro: diagrammatic (Lean `≫`) composition. Defined locally because this
6	% chapter is the writer's only editable file; flagged in the writer report for promotion
7	% to macros/common.tex if desired. \providecommand is a no-op if it is later added globally.
8	\providecommand{\fatsemi}{\mathbin{\,;\,}}
9	
10	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-157). This chapter is the dedicated home of the project's COMMITTED
11	% genus-0 route (route (c)): the characteristic-free abelian-variety rigidity stack. It is
12	% 1:1 with the new upstream Lean file AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean, which
13	% imports AlgebraicJacobian.Genus and is imported by AlgebraicJacobian.Jacobian (breaking the
14	% RigidityKbar -> Rigidity -> Jacobian import cycle so that genusZeroWitness can consume the
15	% keystone directly). The headline thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV is the char-free
16	% replacement (no [CharZero]) for the over-k-bar declaration rigidity_over_kbar of
17	% \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} (which remains in tree as the fallback route (a) artifact and
18	% still carries [CharZero]). The df=0 / H^0(P^1,Omega)=0 / Serre-duality framing is the
19	% fallback (a), NOT the route developed here.
20	
21	The genus-$0$ arm of the Jacobian-witness construction has a \emph{trivial} witness object
22	($J = \Spec k$); its only remaining mathematical content is a rigidity \emph{statement}:
23	every pointed morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus $0$
24	into an abelian variety is the constant morphism at the identity. We prove this
25	\textbf{characteristic-free} --- the protected goal is over an arbitrary field $k$, so the
26	cheaper differential argument (via $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal
27	O(-2)) = 0$, hence $df = 0$, hence $f$ constant) is unavailable: it breaks on a
28	characteristic-$p$ Frobenius-descent wall, where a nonconstant $f$ can have $df = 0$. The
29	rigidity route below sidesteps that wall.
30	
31	The chain has two parts. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, Mumford,
32	Form~I; equivalently Milne's Rigidity Theorem~1.1) is an elementary properness/closed-map
33	argument --- it uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, is the entry point
34	for formalisation, and is now proven axiom-clean. From the Rigidity Lemma, Milne's \S I.3
35	derives the genus-$0$ base case \emph{without} the theorem of the cube --- and, in this
36	project's reading, \emph{without} Milne's Theorem~3.2 (rational-map extension), Lemma~3.3,
37	Auslander--Buchsbaum, or differentials (the ``4th route''; see
38	\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). The decisive observation is that $\mathbb P^1$ is
39	\emph{already complete} and $f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ is \emph{already a total morphism}, so
40	there is no rational defect map to extend over a complete surface and Theorem~3.2 never enters.
41	Instead the base case is closed by the \textbf{$\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut} (the project's
42	primary route, an Archon-original realisation of Milne Proposition~3.9): one uses the
43	\emph{multiplicative scaling} action $\sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to
44	\mathbb P^1$, $(x, \lambda) \mapsto \lambda x$ (the M\"obius scaling fixing both $0$ and
45	$\infty$), which is a \emph{genuine total} scheme morphism on all of $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb
46	G_m$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}). Normalising $f(0) = \eta_A$ and composing $h := \sigma_\times
47	\fatsemi f$, one applies additivity of $\mathrm{Hom}(-, A)$ over a product
48	(\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, Milne Corollary~1.5) --- whose Lean signature requires
49	only the \emph{first} factor complete --- with $V = \mathbb P^1$ proper, $W = \mathbb G_m$, and
50	base points $0 \in \mathbb P^1$, $1 \in \mathbb G_m$. Because $0$ is a \emph{fixed point} of
51	scaling, the $W$-axis restriction $\lambda \mapsto h(0, \lambda) = f(\lambda \cdot 0) = f(0)$ is
52	\emph{constant} at $\eta_A$, so the decomposition collapses to $h = p \fatsemi f$, i.e.\
53	$f(\lambda x) = f(x)$. Specialising $x = 1$ gives that $f|_{\mathbb G_m}$ is constant, and since
54	$\mathbb G_m$ is dense in $\mathbb P^1$ with $A$ separated, $f$ is constant
55	(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}, via the separated-target/dominant-source handle
56	\cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}). This route uses \emph{only} the proven Corollary~1.5, a
57	scaling fixed point, and density --- it never forms the additive defect map, never invokes
58	$\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$, and so sidesteps the ``image of a group homomorphism is a
59	closed subgroup'' input that has no Mathlib support. The classical $\mathbb G_a$-additive
60	argument (\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial} via the $\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m$ incompatibility
61	\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}, the heart of Milne Proposition~3.9) is retained only as the
62	Milne-faithful alternative and is \emph{off} the genus-$0$ critical path. The corollary that a
63	pointed regular map of abelian
64	varieties is a homomorphism (\cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom}, Milne Corollary~1.2) is recorded
65	for completeness and for Route~A but is \emph{not} needed on the genus-$0$ path; Milne's
66	Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) is likewise demoted to a Route-A-only item
67	(the Albanese universal property), no longer on the genus-$0$ critical path
68	(\cref{rmk:thm32_codim1_mathlib_gap}). Combining these
69	gives that every morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant
70	(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}); composing with the classification of genus-$0$
71	curves (\cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}, $C \cong \mathbb P^1$ over $\bar k$) yields the
72	headline \cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}. The theorem of the cube (Milne \S I.5) is
73	\emph{not} used anywhere on this path (see \cref{rmk:cube_not_needed}); the chain invokes
74	neither Serre duality nor the FGA $\Pic$ representability engine, so the genus-$0$ arm is
75	fully decoupled from Route~A.
76	
77	\medskip
78	
79	Throughout, $\bar k$ is an algebraically closed field of arbitrary characteristic. An
80	\emph{abelian variety} over $\bar k$ is a complete (proper) irreducible group variety; in the
81	project's Lean encoding $A$ is an object of $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)$ carrying
82	$\mathtt{GrpObj}$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$, $\mathtt{Smooth}$, and $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$.
83	
84	\section{The Rigidity Lemma}
85	
86	\begin{lemma}
87	
88	\leanok
89	[Rigidity Lemma (Mumford, Form I)]
90	  \label{thm:rigidity_lemma}
91	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_lemma}
92	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
93	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "RIGIDITY LEMMA. (Form I.) Let $X$ be a complete variety, $Y$ and $Z$
94	  % any varieties, and $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ a morphism such that for some
95	  % $y_0 \in Y$, $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0$ of $Z$. Then there is a
96	  % morphism $g \colon Y \to Z$ such that if $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is the
97	  % projection, $f = g \circ p_2$."
98	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43.}
99	  Let $X$ be a complete variety, and let $Y$, $Z$ be any varieties over $\bar k$. Let
100	  $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ be a morphism such that for some $y_0 \in Y$ the image
101	  $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0 \in Z$. Then there is a morphism
102	  $g \colon Y \to Z$ with $f = g \circ p_2$, where $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is the
103	  projection; that is, $f$ depends only on the $Y$-coordinate, and $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$ for any
104	  fixed $x_0 \in X$.
105	\end{lemma}
106	
107	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Choose any point $x_0 \in X$, and define $g \colon Y \to Z$ by
108	% $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$. Since $X \times Y$ is a variety, to show that $f = g \circ p_2$, it is
109	% sufficient to show that these morphisms coincide on some open subset of $X \times Y$. Let
110	% $U$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, $F = Z - U$, and $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$;
111	% then $G$ is closed in $Y$ since $X$ is complete and hence $p_2$ is a closed map. Further
112	% $y_0 \notin G$ since $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}$. Therefore $Y - G = V$ is a non-empty
113	% open subset of $Y$. For each $y \in V$, the complete variety $X \times \{y\}$ gets mapped by
114	% $f$ into the affine variety $U$, and hence to a single point of $U$. But this means that for
115	% any $x \in X$, $y \in V$, $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)$, and this proves our
116	% assertion."
117	\begin{proof}

142	% being LOCALLY OF FINITE TYPE. So [LocallyOfFiniteType (X tensor Y).hom] is now carried on FIVE
143	% chain lemmas (rigidity_lemma, rigidity_core, rigidity_eqOn_dense_open,

148	  Fix any point $x_0 \in X$ and define $g \colon Y \to Z$ by $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$ (the
149	  composite of $y \mapsto (x_0, y)$ with $f$). Since $X \times Y$ is a variety (in
150	  particular reduced and irreducible, so separated and with no embedded structure), to prove
151	  the equality of morphisms $f = g \circ p_2$ it suffices to prove they agree on a non-empty
152	  open subset of $X \times Y$.
153	
154	  Let $U$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, put $F = Z - U$ (a closed set),
155	  and set $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$. Because $X$ is complete, the projection
156	  $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is a \emph{closed} map (this is the defining property of
157	  completeness/properness); hence $G$, the image of the closed set $f^{-1}(F)$, is closed in
158	  $Y$. The hypothesis $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U$ gives
159	  $f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing$, so $y_0 \notin G$. Therefore
160	  $V := Y - G$ is a non-empty open subset of $Y$.
161	
162	  For each $y \in V$ we have $X \times \{y\} \cap f^{-1}(F) = \varnothing$, i.e.
163	  $f(X \times \{y\}) \subseteq U$. Thus the complete (proper) variety $X \times \{y\}$ is
164	  mapped by $f$ into the \emph{affine} variety $U$. A proper connected variety mapping to an
165	  affine variety has image a single point (a global regular function on a complete connected
166	  variety is constant). Hence $f(X \times \{y\})$ is one point, necessarily
167	  $f(x_0, y) = g(y)$. So for all $x \in X$ and $y \in V$,
168	  \[ f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g(y) = (g \circ p_2)(x, y). \]
169	  The two morphisms $f$ and $g \circ p_2$ thus agree on the non-empty open
170	  $X \times V$, which is dense in the irreducible $X \times Y$; as $Z$ is separated they
171	  agree everywhere. \qedhere
172	\end{proof}

174	\begin{remark}
175	  \label{rmk:rigidity_lemma_cube_free}
176	  The proof of \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} uses \emph{only} (i) that completeness of $X$ makes
177	  $p_2$ a closed map, and (ii) that a proper connected variety has no nonconstant map to an
178	  affine variety. It uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, and is valid in
179	  arbitrary characteristic. This is the designated formalisation entry point: it is the
180	  lowest-prerequisite link of the chain. (Mumford gives this as the geometric heart of the
181	  ``second proof'' of commutativity of an abelian variety; Milne packages the same statement
182	  as the Rigidity Theorem~1.1, requiring $V$ complete and $V \times W$ geometrically
183	  irreducible.)
184	\end{remark}

189	  the proof above: \texttt{rigidity\_snd\_lift} (the cartesian-monoidal algebra identifying the
190	  collapsed map $(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$ followed by $f$ with $g \circ p_2$);
191	  \texttt{rigidity\_core} (the scheme-level gluing: two maps out of the geometrically irreducible
192	  reduced $X \times Y$ into the separated $Z$ that agree on a non-empty open subset agree
193	  everywhere, by separatedness); and \texttt{rigidity\_eqOn\_dense\_open}
194	  (\cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}, the genuine geometry: the construction of the non-empty
195	  open $X \times V$ and the slice-constancy of $f$ on it). The collapse hypothesis
196	  $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}$ must be threaded through \emph{all three} helpers, since it is
197	  what makes $V$ non-empty; a decomposition that drops it from the lower helpers is unsound
198	  (see \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}).

1174	  \[ \sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to \mathbb P^1, \qquad

1263	
1264	  Let $A$ be an abelian variety over the algebraically closed field $\bar k$, and let
1265	  $\varphi \colon \mathbb G_a \to A$ be a homomorphism of group objects (so $\varphi(0) = 0_A$ and
1266	  $\varphi(x + y) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y)$). Then $\varphi$ is the trivial homomorphism: it
1267	  factors through the terminal object as the constant morphism at the identity,
1268	  $\varphi = \mathtt{toUnit}\,\mathbb G_a \fatsemi \eta_A$.
1269	
1270	  \medskip
1271	  \noindent\textbf{Equivalent completeness phrasing.} Equivalently: a homomorphism from an
1272	  \emph{affine connected} group object into a \emph{complete} group object is trivial. That is the
1273	  abstract content; the proof below realises it characteristic-free.
1274	\end{lemma}
1275	
1276	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. According to (3.2), α extends to a regular map on the whole of A¹.
1277	% After composing α with a translation, we may suppose that α(0)=0. Then α is a homomorphism,
1278	% α(x+y)=α(x)+α(y); all x,y ∈ A¹(k^al)=k^al. But A¹−{0} is also a group variety, and similarly,
1279	% α(xy)=α(x)+α(y)+c; all x,y ∈ A¹(k^al)=k^al. This is absurd, unless α is constant." [Milne,
1280	% Prop 3.9, \S I.3, doc p.~19--20.]
1281	\begin{proof}
1282	  \uses{lem:hom_additivity_over_product, lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}
1283	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.9.}
1284	  Write $A$ additively (an abelian variety is commutative). We give the conceptual
1285	  \emph{completeness} proof as the abstract argument, then record Milne's elementary $\mathbb
1286	  G_a/\mathbb G_m$ incompatibility, which applies whenever $\varphi$ extends to a morphism on
1287	  $\mathbb P^1$ (as it does in the genus-$0$ application).
1288	
1289	  \textbf{Completeness proof.} The image $\varphi(\mathbb G_a)$ is a connected subgroup of $A$
1290	  (image of the connected $\mathbb G_a$ under a homomorphism). As the image of a homomorphism of
1291	  algebraic groups it is a \emph{closed} subgroup of $A$, hence \emph{complete} (closed in the
1292	  complete $A$); as a homomorphic image of the \emph{affine} $\mathbb G_a$ it is moreover
1293	  \emph{affine}. A connected variety that is simultaneously complete and affine over $\bar k$ is a
1294	  single point: its ring of global regular functions is a field finite over $\bar k$, hence equal
1295	  to $\bar k$ (the $\Gamma = \bar k$ collapse already packaged for proper integral sources in
1296	  \cref{lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}). Therefore $\varphi(\mathbb G_a)$ is one point;
1297	  being a homomorphism, $\varphi$ sends $0$ there, so the point is $0_A$ and
1298	  $\varphi = \mathtt{toUnit}\,\mathbb G_a \fatsemi \eta_A$.
1299	
1300	  \medskip
1301	  \noindent\textbf{Milne's elementary $\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m$ incompatibility (when $\varphi$
1302	  extends to $\mathbb P^1$).} Suppose $\varphi = f|_{\mathbb G_a}$ for a morphism
1303	  $f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ (the genus-$0$ situation). Set $g := f - f(1)$, so $g(1) = 0_A$, and
1304	  form $h_\times := \sigma_\times \fatsemi g \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to A$ with the
1305	  scaling companion $\sigma_\times$ of \cref{def:gaTranslationP1}. Applying the additive
1306	  decomposition \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} with $V = \mathbb P^1$ (complete),
1307	  $W = \mathbb G_m$, base points $1 \in \mathbb P^1$ and $1 \in \mathbb G_m$ (so
1308	  $h_\times(1, 1) = g(1) = 0_A$), the axis restrictions are $x \mapsto g(x)$ and
1309	  $\lambda \mapsto g(\lambda)$, whence $g(\lambda x) = g(x) + g(\lambda)$ on $\bar k^\times$;
1310	  re-expanding $g$ gives the multiplicative relation
1311	  $\varphi(xy) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y) + c$ with $c = -\varphi(1)$. Now $\varphi$ is at once
1312	  additive, $\varphi(x + y) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y)$, and ``multiplicative-additive'',
1313	  $\varphi(xy) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y) + c$. Computing $\varphi((x + x')\,y)$ in two ways for
1314	  $x, x' \in \bar k$, $y \in \bar k^\times$,
1315	  \[ \varphi((x+x')y) = \varphi(xy) + \varphi(x'y) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(x') + 2\varphi(y) + 2c, \]
1316	  while also $\varphi((x+x')y) = \varphi(x+x') + \varphi(y) + c = \varphi(x) + \varphi(x') +
1317	  \varphi(y) + c$; equating gives $\varphi(y) = -c = \varphi(1)$ for every $y \in \bar k^\times$.
1318	  Hence $\varphi$ is constant on the dense $\mathbb G_m \subset \mathbb G_a$, so constant on
1319	  $\mathbb G_a$ (density, $A$ separated), with value $\varphi(0) = 0_A$. This is Milne's argument
1320	  verbatim and uses only the proven \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} plus the scaling action,
1321	  avoiding the ``image is a closed subgroup'' input of the completeness proof. \qedhere
1322	\end{proof}
1323	
1324	\begin{remark}
1325	  \label{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}
1326	  \textbf{The primary route is the $\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut; why no Theorem~3.2 and no
1327	  $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$ are needed.} The genus-$0$ base case ``$f \colon \mathbb P^1
1328	  \to A$ is constant'' is closed \emph{directly} by the proven additive decomposition
1329	  \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} composed with a total $\mathbb G$-action on $\mathbb P^1$,
1330	  with \emph{no} appeal to Milne's Theorem~3.2 / Lemma~3.3 (rational-map extension), the theorem of
1331	  the cube, Auslander--Buchsbaum, or differentials. The reason the earlier draft thought
1332	  Theorem~3.2 was needed --- that the additive defect $\psi(x,y) = f(x+y) - f(x) - f(y)$ lives only
1333	  on the affine $\mathbb G_a \times \mathbb G_a$ and must be extended over $\mathbb P^1 \times
1334	  \mathbb P^1$ --- evaporates once one composes with a \emph{total} action morphism of
1335	  \cref{def:gaTranslationP1}: the composite is already a total morphism out of $\mathbb P^1 \times
1336	  \mathbb G$, and the complete factor is $\mathbb P^1$, exactly what
1337	  \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} (whose Lean signature needs only the first factor
1338	  complete) consumes.
1339	
1340	  \medskip
1341	  \noindent\textbf{Primary route --- the scaling shortcut (strategy decision of iter-164).} The
1342	  \emph{multiplicative} scaling action $\sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to
1343	  \mathbb P^1$, $(x, \lambda) \mapsto \lambda x$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}), closes the base case
1344	  in one shot, \emph{without} the additive homomorphism step or \cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial} at
1345	  all. Normalise $f(0) = 0_A$ (translate; here $0$ is a scaling fixed point). Form $h_\times :=
1346	  \sigma_\times \fatsemi f$ and apply \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} with $V = \mathbb P^1$,
1347	  $W = \mathbb G_m$, base points $0 \in \mathbb P^1$ and $1 \in \mathbb G_m$ (so $h_\times(0, 1) =
1348	  f(0) = 0_A$). Because $0$ is a \emph{fixed point} of scaling, the $W$-axis restriction collapses:
1349	  $\lambda \mapsto h_\times(0, \lambda) = f(\lambda \cdot 0) = f(0) = 0_A$. The decomposition

206	  \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints} (Step~2, ``agree at every closed point of a reduced

219	  type: Step~2 needs the closed points of $U$ to be dense (the Jacobson-space property), which over

233	\begin{lemma}
234	
235	\leanok
236	[Dense-open agreement: the geometric heart of the Rigidity Lemma]
237	  \label{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
238	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
239	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
240	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "RIGIDITY LEMMA. (Form I.) Let $X$ be a complete variety, $Y$ and $Z$
241	  % any varieties, and $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ a morphism such that for some
242	  % $y_0 \in Y$, $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0$ of $Z$. Then there is a
243	  % morphism $g \colon Y \to Z$ such that if $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is the
244	  % projection, $f = g \circ p_2$."
245	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43 (the
246	  dense-open construction is the body of that proof).}
247	  Let $X$ be a complete variety and $Y$, $Z$ any varieties over $\bar k$. Let
248	  $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ be a morphism, fix a point $x_0 \in X$, and suppose given
249	  $y_0 \in Y$, $z_0 \in Z$ with the \emph{collapse hypothesis}
250	  \[ f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \qquad
251	     \text{(in Lean: } \mathtt{lift}\,(\mathbf 1_X)\,(\mathtt{toUnit}\,X \fatsemi y_0) \fatsemi f
252	     = \mathtt{toUnit}\,X \fatsemi z_0\text{).} \]
253	  Then there is a non-empty open subset $U \subseteq X \times Y$ on which $f$ agrees, as a
254	  scheme morphism, with the collapsed map $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$, where
255	  $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,(\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,
256	  (p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y)$ is the ``$(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$'' endomorphism. Concretely
257	  $U = X \times V$ with $V := Y - G$, $G = p_2(f^{-1}(Z - U_0))$ for an affine open
258	  neighbourhood $U_0$ of $z_0$, and on $U$ one has $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y)$.
259	
260	  \medskip
261	  \noindent\textbf{The collapse hypothesis is load-bearing.} The non-emptiness of $V$ is exactly
262	  the assertion $y_0 \notin G$, and this holds \emph{because} $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}
263	  \subseteq U_0$ keeps the slice over $y_0$ inside the affine $U_0$, away from $F = Z - U_0$. A
264	  version of this lemma \emph{without} the collapse hypothesis is false: for $X = Y = Z$ and
265	  $f = p_1$ the first projection, $f$ and $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f \colon (x, y) \mapsto x_0$
266	  agree on no non-empty open (the agreement locus is contained in $\{x = x_0\}$, which has empty
267	  interior in the irreducible $X \times Y$). Therefore the formal target \emph{must} carry the
268	  collapse hypothesis as an explicit antecedent; dropping it (as an earlier decomposition
269	  silently did) makes the deferred goal unsatisfiable.
270	
271	  \medskip
272	  \noindent\textbf{Formalization note: algebraically closed base, and locally-of-finite-type
273	  source.} The prose already takes $\bar k$ algebraically closed, but the formal statement must
274	  \emph{encode} two properties. First, the cohomology-free proof of the slice-constancy bridge
275	  (below) needs each per-closed-point residue field $\kappa(y)$ to be $\bar k$ itself, so that a
276	  proper integral slice has global sections $\Gamma = \bar k$ and maps to a single $\bar k$-point of
277	  the affine $U_0$; this requires $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$. Second, the globalisation of the
278	  per-slice agreement (Step~2 of the bridge, \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}) needs the
279	  closed points of the saturated open $U$ to be \emph{dense} --- i.e.\ $U$ to be a Jacobson space.
280	  Over the algebraically closed $\bar k$ this holds precisely because $X \times Y$ is \emph{locally of
281	  finite type} over $\bar k$ (\texttt{LocallyOfFiniteType.jacobsonSpace} transports the Jacobson
282	  property of $\Spec \bar k$, and \texttt{closure\_closedPoints} then gives density); this requires
283	  $[\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]$. Accordingly the formalization carries
284	  \emph{both} $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$ and
285	  $[\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]$ on this lemma (in addition to
286	  $[\mathtt{Field}\ \bar k]$), and propagates the same two instances to \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma},
287	  its core helper \texttt{rigidity\_core}, and the bridge sub-lemmas
288	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} and
289	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}. Both cost nothing downstream: the
290	  consumers \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} and \cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} (Lean
291	  \texttt{rigidity\_genus0\_curve\_to\_grpScheme}) \emph{already} assume
292	  $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$ --- the base-field variable is literally named $\mathtt{kbar}$ ---
293	  and curves and abelian varieties are of finite type over $\bar k$, so the
294	  locally-of-finite-type instance is automatic there.
295	\end{lemma}
296	
297	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Choose any point $x_0 \in X$, and define $g \colon Y \to Z$ by
298	% $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$. Since $X \times Y$ is a variety, to show that $f = g \circ p_2$, it is
299	% sufficient to show that these morphisms coincide on some open subset of $X \times Y$. Let
300	% $U$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, $F = Z - U$, and $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$;
301	% then $G$ is closed in $Y$ since $X$ is complete and hence $p_2$ is a closed map. Further
302	% $y_0 \notin G$ since $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}$. Therefore $Y - G = V$ is a non-empty
303	% open subset of $Y$. For each $y \in V$, the complete variety $X \times \{y\}$ gets mapped by
304	% $f$ into the affine variety $U$, and hence to a single point of $U$. But this means that for
305	% any $x \in X$, $y \in V$, $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)$, and this proves our
306	% assertion."
307	\begin{proof}
308	  \uses{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine}
309	  \leanok
310	  Let $U_0$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, put $F = Z - U_0$, and set
311	  $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$. Because $X$ is complete, the projection $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is a
312	  \emph{closed} map, so $G$ (the image of the closed set $f^{-1}(F)$) is closed in $Y$. The
313	  collapse hypothesis $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U_0$ gives
314	  $f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing$, hence $y_0 \notin G$; this is the only place
315	  the collapse hypothesis is used, and it is essential, for $V := Y - G$ is non-empty precisely
316	  because $y_0 \in V$. For each $y \in V$ the proper variety $X \times \{y\}$ is mapped by $f$
317	  into the affine $U_0$, hence to a single point, necessarily $f(x_0, y)$. So for all $x \in X$,
318	  $y \in V$, we have $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x, y)$, i.e.\ $f$ and
319	  $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$ agree on the non-empty open $U := X \times V$. (In the Lean
320	  encoding the two slice-constancy bridges are the closed-map step from properness and the
321	  ``proper-into-affine has single-point image'' step; see the docstring of
322	  \texttt{rigidity\_core}.)
323	
324	  \medskip
325	  \noindent\textbf{Formalization notes (the concrete char-free Mathlib route).} The textbook
326	  proof above is realised in Lean by three pieces. They cite Mathlib lemma names as the chosen
327	  route; none uses cohomology, and the relative Stein-factorisation framing is deliberately
328	  \emph{avoided} (see below).
329	
330	  \emph{Bridge~1 (the closed-map step) --- BUILT.} ``$X$ complete $\Rightarrow p_2$ closed'' is
331	  the axiom-clean helper \texttt{snd\_left\_isClosedMap} (iter-158). It is obtained from
332	  \texttt{IsProper.toUniversallyClosed} on $X$, base-changed across the canonical pullback square
333	  to make $p_2$ universally closed, using that the monoidal projection \emph{is} the chosen
334	  pullback projection: \texttt{Over.snd\_left} rewrites $(\mathtt{snd}\,X\,Y).\mathtt{left}$ to
335	  $\mathtt{Limits.pullback.snd}\ X.\mathtt{hom}\ Y.\mathtt{hom}$ exactly. This step is already
336	  proven.
337	
338	  \emph{The fibre fact (non-emptiness of $V$) --- RESOLVED route, char-free.} That the slice
339	  $X \times \{y_0\}$ over the $\bar k$-rational point $y_0$ is exactly the image of the slice
340	  section $s \colon X \to X \times Y$ is formalised \emph{without} the residue-field /
341	  \texttt{Triplet} / tensor-product machinery (that fine \texttt{carrierEquiv} layer is the wrong
342	  granularity and is to be avoided). One pastes the identity square for the section against the
343	  canonical pullback square (\texttt{IsPullback.of\_right},
344	  \texttt{IsPullback.of\_horiz\_isIso}; the section identities $s \fatsemi p_1 = \mathbf 1$ and
345	  $s \fatsemi p_2 = X.\mathtt{hom} \fatsemi y_0$ come from \texttt{lift\_fst}/\texttt{lift\_snd}
346	  with \texttt{Over.w} on the unit) and reads off the fibre with the \emph{coarse} topological
347	  layer of \texttt{PullbackCarrier}, namely
348	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pullback.image\_preimage\_eq\_of\_isPullback}. This needs no
349	  $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}]$.
350	
351	  \emph{Bridge~2 (slice-constancy / the agreement equation) --- RESOLVED route, cohomology-FREE.}
352	  ``Proper connected slice into affine $\Rightarrow$ single point, glued to a morphism equality on
353	  $U$'' is realised by a \textbf{global-sections + per-closed-point} route, \emph{not} by any
354	  relative Stein-factorisation or proper-pushforward $f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O$ statement: the
355	  latter is a genuine Mathlib gap (no Stein factorisation, no $H^0$ base change), and route~(c)
356	  exists precisely to avoid coherent cohomology, so it must \emph{not} be attempted. The concrete
357	  stack is: for each closed point $y \in V$ one has $\kappa(y) = \bar k$ (alg-closed, finite type);
358	  the slice $X_y$ is proper integral, so its global sections form a field
359	  (\texttt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}) module-finite over $\bar k$
360	  (\texttt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}), and algebraic closedness kills the finite
361	  extension to give $\Gamma(X_y) = \bar k$. A morphism into the affine $U_0$ is pinned by its ring
362	  map on global sections (\texttt{ext\_of\_isAffine}), so $f$ and
363	  $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$ agree on each closed slice. Closed points are dense in the
364	  locally-of-finite-type $U$ (\texttt{closure\_closedPoints} / the Jacobson-space property of
365	  locally-of-finite-type $\bar k$-schemes; in the formalization this density is supplied by the new
366	  hypothesis $[\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]$), so the per-slice
367	  agreement globalises to all of $U$ via the reduced-source / separated-target rigidity
368	  \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (the same handle \texttt{rigidity\_core} already
369	  uses). The packaged ``morphisms agreeing on a dense set of closed points are equal''
370	  hom-extensionality --- the one connective Mathlib does not supply directly (it gives only the
371	  single-dominant-morphism \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant}) --- has now been built as the axiom-clean
372	  \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints} (Step~2): it assembles the closed points into one
373	  dominant coproduct probe and applies \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant}. The whole argument is extracted
374	  into the dedicated obligation \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} below, whose body
375	  wires that proven Step~2 over the per-slice Step~1
376	  (\cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}); Step~1 (Mumford's per-closed-slice
377	  constancy) is now the chain's \emph{single} genuinely-deep residual \texttt{sorry}.
378	\end{proof}
