380	\begin{lemma}
381	
382	\leanok
383	[Bridge~2: slice-constancy on a saturated open mapping into an affine]
384	  \label{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine}
385	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine}
386	  % NOTE (iter-161): the iter-160 SIGNATURE GAP is RESOLVED. The Lean proof wires Step 2
387	  % (morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints, PROVEN axiom-clean) over Step 1
388	  % (rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine, the residual sorry). Step 2 needs the closed
389	  % points of U to be DENSE, i.e. U Jacobson; over k̄ this follows from (X⊗Y) being locally of finite
390	  % type, so [LocallyOfFiniteType (X⊗Y).hom] is now a stated hypothesis of this lemma (and across the
391	  % chain). With it, the `JacobsonSpace U` instance is a routine discharge (LocallyOfFiniteType.
392	  % jacobsonSpace + JacobsonSpace.of_isOpenEmbedding), no longer an as-typed gap. The only `sorry`
393	  % reachable from this lemma is now Step 1 (Mumford's per-closed-slice constancy). The hypothesis
394	  % set is recorded in the statement and proof prose below (both [IsAlgClosed k̄] AND
395	  % [LocallyOfFiniteType (X⊗Y).hom]); the earlier "[IsAlgClosed] is the only added instance" claim
396	  % is retired.
397	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
398	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "For each $y \in V$, the complete variety $X \times \{y\}$ gets mapped by
399	  % $f$ into the affine variety $U$, and hence to a single point of $U$. But this means that for
400	  % any $x \in X$, $y \in V$, $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)$, and this proves our
401	  % assertion." [Mumford, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma, p.~43.]
402	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43 (the
403	  ``for each $y \in V$, the complete slice maps into the affine, hence to a single point'' step).}
404	  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and let $X$, $Y$, $Z$ be objects of
405	  $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)$ with $X$ proper, $X \times Y$ geometrically irreducible, reduced,
406	  and \emph{locally of finite type} over $\bar k$ (the formalization carries
407	  $[\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]$, which makes the saturated open $U$
408	  below a Jacobson space, so its closed points are dense --- the hypothesis Step~2 of the proof
409	  consumes), and $Z$ separated. Let $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ be a morphism, fix a $\bar k$-point
410	  $x_0 \colon \mathbf 1 \to X$, and write $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,
411	  (\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,(p_2)$ for the ``$(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$''
412	  endomorphism. Suppose:
413	  \begin{itemize}
414	    \item $U \subseteq X \times Y$ is a $p_2$-\emph{saturated} open, i.e.\ $U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})$
415	      for a set $V_{\mathrm{set}} \subseteq Y$ (so $U$ contains the whole fibre $X_y$ over each of
416	      its points);
417	    \item $U_0 \subseteq Z$ is an \emph{affine} open;
418	    \item $f(U) \subseteq U_0$, i.e.\ $f$ maps $U$ into the affine $U_0$.
419	  \end{itemize}
420	  Then $f$ and $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$ agree on $U$ as scheme morphisms:
421	  $U.\iota \fatsemi f = U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)$.
422	\end{lemma}
423	
424	\begin{proof}
425	
426	  \uses{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints, lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}
427	  \leanok
428	  This is the formalization realization of Mumford's step ``for each $y \in V$, the complete
429	  variety $X \times \{y\}$ gets mapped by $f$ into the affine variety $U$, and hence to a single
430	  point of $U$''. We realise it by a \textbf{global-sections + per-closed-point} route that is
431	  \emph{cohomology-free}; in particular the relative Stein-factorisation / proper-pushforward
432	  $f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O$ framing is a confirmed Mathlib gap (no Stein factorisation, no
433	  $H^0$ base change) and is \emph{deliberately avoided}. The argument splits into two named
434	  sub-lemmas, one residual and one proven.
435	
436	  \medskip
437	  \noindent\textbf{Step 1 (per-closed-slice constancy) --- \cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}, the residual.}
438	  Fix a closed point $x$ of $U$, lying over a closed point $y = p_2(x) \in V_{\mathrm{set}}$.
439	  Since $\bar k$ is algebraically closed and $Y$ is of finite type over $\bar k$, the residue field
440	  is $\kappa(y) = \bar k$. Because $U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})$ is saturated, $U$ contains the
441	  \emph{entire} fibre $X_y \cong X$ over $y$; hence $f$ maps the proper integral slice $X_y$ into the
442	  affine $U_0$. By $\mathtt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}$ the global sections $\Gamma(X_y)$ form a
443	  field, module-finite over $\bar k$ by $\mathtt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}$, and
444	  algebraic closedness collapses the finite extension to give $\Gamma(X_y) = \bar k$. A morphism into
445	  the affine $U_0$ is pinned by its ring map on global sections ($\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}$), so the
446	  restriction of $f$ to $X_y$ factors through a single $\bar k$-point of $U_0$ --- necessarily the
447	  point $f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x)$. Thus $f$ and $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$
448	  agree (after the residue-field probe) at every closed point of $U$. This per-slice statement is the
449	  chain's single genuinely-deep residual \texttt{sorry}.
450	
451	  \medskip
452	  \noindent\textbf{Step 2 (globalisation over dense closed points) --- \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}, proven.}
453	  The closed points are dense in the locally-of-finite-type $\bar k$-scheme $U$
454	  ($\mathtt{closure\_closedPoints}$ / the Jacobson-space property, supplied here by the new hypothesis
455	  $[\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]$). To turn ``$f$ and
456	  $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$ agree at each closed point'' into a single morphism equality on $U$,
457	  the proven globalisation \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints} assembles the closed points
458	  into one dominant probe --- the coproduct
459	  $\coprod_{x \in \mathrm{closedPoints}\,U} \Spec \kappa(x) \to U$, whose range is dense --- and feeds
460	  it to the reduced-source / separated-target rigidity handle $\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}$ (the same
461	  family $\mathtt{rigidity\_core}$ uses). This yields the morphism equality
462	  $U.\iota \fatsemi f = U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)$ on all of $U$. The
463	  ``agrees on a dense set of closed points $\Rightarrow$ hom-equality'' connective is the one piece
464	  Mathlib does not package directly (it supplies only the single-dominant-morphism
465	  $\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}$); that bespoke build is now done in
466	  \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}, and it is \emph{not} cohomology. \qedhere
467	\end{proof}

469	\begin{lemma}\leanok
470	[Step~2: morphisms agreeing at every closed point of a Jacobson source are equal]
471	  \label{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}
472	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}
473	  % Project-bespoke assembly: NO external source. Mathlib supplies only the single-dominant-morphism
474	  % ext_of_isDominant; the "dense closed points => hom-extensionality" connective is built here.
475	  Let $W$, $Z$ be schemes with $W$ \emph{reduced}, $W$ a \emph{Jacobson space}, and $Z$
476	  \emph{separated}. Let $g_1, g_2 \colon W \to Z$ be morphisms. If, for every \emph{closed} point
477	  $x \in W$, the two morphisms agree after restriction along the residue-field point
478	  $\Spec \kappa(x) \to W$ (in Lean, $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_1 =
479	  W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_2$), then $g_1 = g_2$.
480	\end{lemma}
481	
482	\begin{proof}
483	
484	\leanok
485	  This is the ``dense closed points $\Rightarrow$ hom-extensionality'' connective that Mathlib does
486	  not package directly: it supplies only the single-dominant-morphism extensionality
487	  $\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}$ (a dominant morphism into a separated target is an epimorphism for
488	  the purpose of right-cancellation). We assemble the per-closed-point hypotheses into one such
489	  dominant probe.
490	
491	  Form the coproduct $P := \coprod_{x \in \mathrm{closedPoints}\,W} \Spec \kappa(x)$ and the morphism
492	  $\mathrm{probe} \colon P \to W$ defined by $\mathtt{Sigma.desc}$ of the residue-field points
493	  $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x$. Its topological range contains every closed point of $W$:
494	  the inclusion $\iota_x \colon \Spec \kappa(x) \to P$ composes with $\mathrm{probe}$ to
495	  $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x$ (the defining property of $\mathtt{Sigma.desc}$), and the
496	  range of $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x$ is the singleton $\{x\}$
497	  ($\mathtt{Scheme.range\_fromSpecResidueField}$). Because $W$ is a Jacobson space, the closed points
498	  are dense ($\mathtt{closure\_closedPoints}$, i.e.\ $\overline{\mathrm{closedPoints}\,W} = W$), so
499	  the range of $\mathrm{probe}$ is dense and $\mathrm{probe}$ is \emph{dominant}.

500	
501	  Componentwise, $g_1$ and $g_2$ agree after $\mathrm{probe}$: precomposing with each coproduct
502	  inclusion $\iota_x$ reduces (by $\mathtt{Sigma.\iota\_desc}$) to
503	  $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_1 = W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_2$,
504	  which is the hypothesis at the closed point $x$. By the universal property of the coproduct
505	  ($\mathtt{Sigma.hom\_ext}$) this gives $\mathrm{probe} \fatsemi g_1 = \mathrm{probe} \fatsemi g_2$.
506	  Since $\mathrm{probe}$ is dominant and $Z$ is separated, $\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}$ cancels it
507	  on the left to yield $g_1 = g_2$.
508	\end{proof}

510	\begin{lemma}
511	
512	\leanok
513	[Global-sections constancy: two $\bar k$-points of a proper integral source into an affine agree]
514	  \label{lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}
515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}
516	  % Project-bespoke assembly of Mathlib lemmas: NO external source. Realises, cohomology-free, the
517	  % classical "a global regular function on a proper integral variety over an algebraically closed
518	  % field is constant" --- precisely the affine-image step of Mumford's Rigidity Lemma proof quoted
519	  % above (the "complete slice into an affine maps to a single point" sentence, Ch.~II \S4, p.~43).
520	  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and let $W$ be an integral scheme equipped with a structure
521	  morphism $w \colon W \to \Spec \bar k$ that is universally closed (proper) and locally of finite
522	  type. Let $g \colon W \to V$ be a morphism into an \emph{affine} scheme $V$. Then any two
523	  $\bar k$-points of $W$ --- i.e.\ any two sections $a, b \colon \Spec \bar k \to W$ of $w$ (so
524	  $a \fatsemi w = \mathbf 1$ and $b \fatsemi w = \mathbf 1$) --- satisfy $a \fatsemi g = b \fatsemi g$.
525	
526	  \medskip
527	  \noindent\textbf{Every hypothesis is load-bearing} (confirmed by the iter-161 audit).
528	  $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$ collapses the finite extension $\Gamma(W)/\bar k$; integrality of
529	  $W$ makes $\Gamma(W)$ a domain (without it a two-point disjoint union is a counterexample);
530	  $\mathtt{UniversallyClosed}$ is what upgrades $\Gamma(W)$ to a \emph{field} (without it
531	  $\mathbb A^1$, with $\Gamma = \bar k[t]$ and two distinct $\bar k$-points, is a counterexample);
532	  $\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}$ gives finiteness of $\Gamma(W)$ over $\bar k$; and
533	  $[\mathtt{IsAffine}\ V]$ is what lets $\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}$ pin the morphism by its ring map
534	  on global sections.
535	\end{lemma}
536	
537	\begin{proof}
538	
539	\leanok
540	  Because $W$ is integral and $w$ is universally closed, the global sections
541	  $\Gamma(W, \mathcal O_W)$ form a field ($\mathtt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}$), and the structure
542	  ring map $w^\sharp \colon \bar k \to \Gamma(W)$ (the map $\mathtt{wk.appTop}$ after the canonical
543	  identification $\Gamma(\Spec \bar k) \cong \bar k$) is integral / module-finite
544	  ($\mathtt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}$). Since $\bar k$ is algebraically closed and
545	  $\Gamma(W)$ is a domain finite over it, that map is bijective
546	  ($\mathtt{IsAlgClosed.ringHom\_bijective\_of\_isIntegral}$); thus $\Gamma(W) = \bar k$ and
547	  $w^\sharp$ is an isomorphism on global sections --- in particular an epimorphism. Both sections
548	  $a$, $b$ left-invert $w^\sharp$ on global sections (they are sections of $w$), so they induce the
549	  \emph{same} ring map $a^\sharp = b^\sharp$ on $\Gamma(W)$ (cancel the epimorphism $w^\sharp$). A
550	  morphism into the \emph{affine} $V$ is pinned by its global-sections ring map
551	  ($\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}$), whence $a \fatsemi g = b \fatsemi g$. This realises, without
552	  cohomology, the classical ``proper connected variety into an affine is a single point'' step
553	  quoted from Mumford above.
554	\end{proof}

557	
558	\leanok
559	[Integrality descends to a retract of an integral scheme]
560	  \label{lem:isIntegral_of_retract_of_integral}
561	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isIntegral_of_retract}
562	  % NOTE: (iter-162 review) project-bespoke (elementary topology + algebra); NO external source.
563	  % LANDED axiom-clean as `AlgebraicGeometry.isIntegral_of_retract`, stated generically for any
564	  % retract `r : S ⟶ T`, `pr : T ⟶ S` with `r ≫ pr = 𝟙 S` of an integral `T` (more general than
565	  % the `X`/`X×Y`/`p_1` instance below). PROOF DIVERGENCE (cosmetic, sound either way): the Lean
566	  % proves reducedness PER-STALK (each `S.stalk x` embeds via `pr.stalkMap` into the reduced
567	  % `T.stalk`, then `isReduced_of_isReduced_stalk`), not via the global-sections split-injection
568	  % the prose below describes. Plan/blueprint-writer may align the prose to the stalk route.
569	  Let $X \times Y$ be an integral scheme (here it is geometrically irreducible and reduced over
570	  $\bar k$). Suppose $X$ is a \emph{retract} of $X \times Y$: there is a section
571	  $s \colon X \to X \times Y$ of the first projection $p_1$, i.e.\ $s \fatsemi p_1 = \mathbf 1_X$.
572	  Then $X$ is integral.
573	\end{lemma}
574	
575	\begin{proof}
576	
577	\leanok
578	  Integrality is reducedness together with irreducibility, and the retract supplies each.
579	
580	  \emph{Reduced.} The section identity $s \fatsemi p_1 = \mathbf 1_X$ makes the pullback on global
581	  sections $p_1^\sharp \colon \mathcal O_X(X) \to \mathcal O_{X \times Y}(X \times Y)$ split
582	  injective (it is left-inverted by $s^\sharp$). A split-injective ring map into the reduced ring
583	  $\mathcal O_{X \times Y}(X \times Y)$ embeds $\mathcal O_X(X)$ into a reduced ring, so it too is
584	  reduced ($\mathtt{isReduced\_of\_injective}$ on the split injection); hence $X$ is reduced.
585	
586	  \emph{Irreducible.} The first projection $p_1 \colon X \times Y \to X$ is surjective because it
587	  admits the section $s$ (every point of $X$ is $p_1(s(\cdot))$). The continuous image of an
588	  irreducible space under a surjection is irreducible; as $X \times Y$ is irreducible and $p_1$ is a
589	  continuous surjection onto $X$, the space of $X$ is irreducible.
590	
591	  Reduced and irreducible together give that $X$ is integral. This is elementary --- no cohomology,
592	  no external citation --- and feeds the Step-1 geometric assembly below, where the proper slice
593	  $X_y \cong X$ must be presented as proper \emph{integral}.
594	\end{proof}

596	\begin{lemma}\leanok

619	\begin{proof}
620	  \uses{lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral, lem:isIntegral_of_retract_of_integral}
621	  \leanok
622	  This is Mumford's per-slice step, realised \emph{cohomology-free} (route~B): the relative Stein /
623	  proper-pushforward $f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O$ framing is a confirmed Mathlib gap and is
624	  deliberately avoided. The closed point $x$ of $U$ lies over a closed point
625	  $y = p_2(x) \in V_{\mathrm{set}}$; since $\bar k$ is algebraically closed and $Y$ is of finite type
626	  over $\bar k$, the residue field is $\kappa(y) = \bar k$. Saturation $U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})$
627	  puts the \emph{entire} proper integral fibre $X_y \cong X$ over $y$ inside $U$, so $f$ maps $X_y$
628	  into the affine $U_0$. Because $X_y$ is proper integral over $\bar k$, its global sections form a
629	  field ($\mathtt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}$), module-finite over $\bar k$
630	  ($\mathtt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}$); algebraic closedness collapses the finite
631	  extension, giving $\Gamma(X_y, \mathcal O) = \bar k$.
632	  % NOTE (iter-162): the iter-161 documentary gap is RESOLVED --- this algebraic core ("two k-bar
633	  % points of a proper integral l.f.t. k-bar-scheme into an affine agree") now has its own node
634	  % lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral (above), and this proof's \uses{} edge points at it.
635	  A morphism into the affine $U_0$ is pinned by
636	  its ring map on global sections ($\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}$), so $f|_{X_y}$ factors through a
637	  single $\bar k$-point of $U_0$. That point is $f(x_0, y)$, since the $\bar k$-point $(x_0, y)$ lies
638	  on $X_y$; and $f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x)$ by the definition of $\mathtt{retract}$.
639	  Evaluating at the residue-field probe of the closed point $x$ gives the asserted equality. This
640	  per-slice statement is the chain's single genuinely-deep residual \texttt{sorry}; its globalisation
641	  over the dense closed points is \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}.
642	\end{proof}
643	
644	\section{The Milne \S I.3 chain: additivity, homomorphisms, extension}
645	
646	This section assembles, from the proven Rigidity Lemma, the genus-$0$ base case. The two
647	load-bearing ingredients of the \textbf{primary route} are the additive decomposition over a
648	product (\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, Milne Corollary~1.5) and the concrete $\mathbb
649	G_m$-scaling action $\sigma_\times$ on $\mathbb P^1$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}); from these
650	the base case ``every morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant'' falls out \emph{in one shot}
651	(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}): the scaling fixed point $0$ collapses one axis of the
652	decomposition, giving $f(\lambda x) = f(x)$ and hence constancy on the dense $\mathbb G_m$. The
653	classical $\mathbb G_a$-additive homomorphism step
654	(\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}) and the triviality lemma $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$
655	(\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}) are retained as the Milne-faithful alternative but are
656	\emph{not} on the genus-$0$ critical path. None of this uses the theorem of the cube, and --- crucially
657	--- none of it uses Milne's Theorem~3.2 / Lemma~3.3 (the rational-map extension): because $f
658	\colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ is \emph{already} a total morphism out of the complete $\mathbb
659	P^1$, there is no defect map to extend over a complete surface (see
660	\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). Corollary~1.2 (\cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom}) and
661	Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) are retained in this chapter only as
662	Route-A inputs (the Albanese universal property) and are explicitly \emph{off} the genus-$0$
663	path. The genus-$0$ output is exactly the content of Milne's Proposition~3.9, ``every rational
664	(here: regular) map $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant''.
665	
666	\begin{remark}
667	  \label{rmk:cube_not_needed}
668	  \textbf{The theorem of the cube is not needed on the genus-$0$ path.} An earlier draft of
669	  this chapter recorded the theorem of the cube as a deferred prerequisite of the single-curve
670	  base case ``$\mathbb P^1 \to A$ constant''. That was a misreading. The base case
671	  (Proposition~3.9, the $d = 1$ case of Proposition~3.10) follows directly from the Rigidity
672	  Theorem~1.1 --- our \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} --- via Corollary~1.5 (additivity,
673	  \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}) composed with the total $\mathbb G_m$-scaling
674	  action $\sigma_\times$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}): the scaling fixed point $0$ collapses one
675	  axis of the decomposition, yielding $f(\lambda x) = f(x)$ and hence constancy on the dense
676	  $\mathbb G_m$ (the primary $\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut; see
677	  \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). This route does not even use the triviality of
678	  $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A)$ (\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}), which is retained only for the
679	  Milne-faithful $\mathbb G_a$-additive alternative. Neither the theorem of the cube nor Milne's Theorem~3.2 /
680	  Lemma~3.3 (rational-map extension) is used: since $f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ is already a
681	  total morphism out of the complete $\mathbb P^1$, no defect map needs extending over a complete
682	  surface (this corrects the earlier claim that the surface extension was on the genus-$0$ path;
683	  see \cref{rmk:thm32_codim1_mathlib_gap}). The theorem of the cube (Milne \S I.5) enters only for
684	  projectivity, the seesaw/square principle, dual abelian varieties, polarizations, and the
685	  Poincar\'e sheaf --- none on the genus-$0$ path. For Route~A: the Albanese universal property
686	  that the positive-genus arm needs (Milne Proposition~6.1/6.4, \S III.6) is \emph{also} cube-free,
687	  resting on Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) + Corollary~1.2/1.5 --- which is
688	  precisely why Theorem~3.2 and Corollary~1.2 are retained in this chapter as Route-A inputs.
689	\end{remark}
690	
691	\begin{lemma}
692	
693	\leanok
694	[Additive decomposition over a product (Milne Corollary 1.5)]
695	  \label{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}
696	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.hom_additive_decomp_of_rigidity}
697	  \uses{thm:rigidity_lemma}
698	  % NOTE: (iter-163 review) The Lean signature carries three product-level instance
699	  % hypotheses on V⊗W not spelled out in the prose: [GeometricallyIrreducible (V⊗W).hom],
700	  % [LocallyOfFiniteType (V⊗W).hom], [IsReduced (V⊗W).left] — i.e. "V×W is a variety".
701	  % These are mathematically free for genuine complete varieties V, W, but Mathlib does not
702	  % auto-derive the product instances from the factors, so they are carried explicitly to keep
703	  % the lemma axiom-clean. Also: the Lean states the EXISTENCE half only (canonical
704	  % axis-restriction witnesses inlined; uniqueness not formalized) and requires only V (not W)
705	  % complete — strictly more general than the prose. (lean-vs-blueprint-checker avr-iter163.)
706	  % NOTE: (iter-164 review) iter-164 hygiene dropped the A-side instances [Smooth A.hom] and
707	  % [GeometricallyIrreducible A.hom] from the Lean signature; the abelian-variety target now
708	  % requires only [GrpObj A] [IsProper A.hom]. The Lean signature is therefore STRICTLY MORE
709	  % GENERAL than the prose ("Let A be an abelian variety"): every situation the prose covers is
710	  % covered, and additionally any proper group object suffices. Axiom-clean preserved.
711	  % (lean-vs-blueprint-checker avr-iter164.)
712	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Corollary 1.5, \S I.1, doc p.~9 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 15)
713	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "COROLLARY 1.5. Let V and W be complete varieties over k with k-rational
714	  % points v0 and w0, and let p and q be the projection maps V×W→V and V×W→W. Let A be an
715	  % abelian variety. Then a morphism h:V×W→A such that h(v0,w0)=0 can be written uniquely as
716	  % h = f∘p + g∘q with f:V→A and g:W→A morphisms such that f(v0)=0 and g(w0)=0."
717	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Corollary~1.5.}
718	  Let $V$, $W$ be complete varieties over $\bar k$ with $\bar k$-points $v_0 \in V$,
719	  $w_0 \in W$, and let $p \colon V \times W \to V$, $q \colon V \times W \to W$ be the
720	  projections. Let $A$ be an abelian variety over $\bar k$. Then a morphism
721	  $h \colon V \times W \to A$ with $h(v_0, w_0) = 0$ can be written uniquely as
722	  $h = f \circ p + g \circ q$, with morphisms $f \colon V \to A$, $g \colon W \to A$ satisfying
723	  $f(v_0) = 0$ and $g(w_0) = 0$.
724	
725	  \emph{Lean encoding.} In the project $A$ is a group object \texttt{[GrpObj A]} (not assumed
726	  commutative). The ``$+$'' here is the binary operation that \texttt{GrpObj} induces on each
727	  hom-set $\mathrm{Hom}(X, A)$: for $u, v \colon X \to A$, $u + v$ is the composite
728	  $X \xrightarrow{\langle u, v\rangle} A \times A \xrightarrow{\;\mathrm{mul}\;} A$ (and
729	  $f \circ p + g \circ q$ is built this way from the projections). The decomposition is therefore
730	  read \emph{multiplicatively} as $h = (f \circ p) \cdot (g \circ q)$ in the group
731	  $\mathrm{Hom}(V \times W, A)$, and does \emph{not} require $A$ commutative; commutativity of
732	  abelian varieties (Milne Cor 1.4, a separate consequence of \cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom})
733	  is needed only to rewrite the product symmetrically/additively, not for the statement or its
734	  proof. The prover should infer the hom-set group operation from the \texttt{GrpObj} instance,
735	  not from a \texttt{CommGrpObj} instance.
736	\end{lemma}
737	
738	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Set f=h|V×{w0}, g=h|{v0}×W, and identify V×{w0} and {v0}×W with
739	% V and W. On points, f(v) = h(v,w0) and g(w) = h(v0,w), and so φ =df h − (f∘p + g∘q) is the
740	% map that sends (v,w) ↦ h(v,w) − h(v,w0) − h(v0,w). Thus φ(V×{w0}) = 0 = φ({v0}×W) and so the
741	% theorem shows that φ = 0." [Milne, Cor 1.5, \S I.1, doc p.~9--10.]
742	\begin{proof}
743	  \uses{thm:rigidity_lemma}
744	  \leanok
745	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Corollary~1.5.}
746	  Define $f := h|_{V \times \{w_0\}}$ and $g := h|_{\{v_0\} \times W}$, identifying
747	  $V \times \{w_0\} \cong V$ and $\{v_0\} \times W \cong W$; on points $f(v) = h(v, w_0)$ and
748	  $g(w) = h(v_0, w)$. Form the difference
749	  \[ \varphi := h - (f \circ p + g \circ q) \colon V \times W \to A, \qquad
750	     \varphi(v, w) = h(v, w) - h(v, w_0) - h(v_0, w) \]
751	  (the subtraction is in the group $A$, i.e.\ $\varphi$ is the composite of
752	  $(h, -(f \circ p + g \circ q))$ with the group law of $A$). Then $\varphi$ vanishes on both
753	  axes: $\varphi(V \times \{w_0\}) = 0 = \varphi(\{v_0\} \times W)$ (direct computation, using
754	  $h(v_0, w_0) = 0$). In particular $\varphi$ collapses the complete axis $V \times \{w_0\}$ to
755	  the single point $0 \in A$, so by the Rigidity Lemma (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, with $V$
756	  complete and $V \times W$ geometrically irreducible) $\varphi$ factors through the projection
757	  $q$; but it also vanishes on $\{v_0\} \times W$, which is a section of $q$, so the factoring
758	  morphism is identically $0$, i.e.\ $\varphi \equiv 0$. Hence $h = f \circ p + g \circ q$.
759	  Uniqueness: restricting any such decomposition to the axes recovers $f$ and $g$ as the
760	  axis-restrictions of $h$.
761	\end{proof}

769	  \uses{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}

771	  % (η[A] ≫ α = η[B] ⟹ IsMonHom α), not the full "composite of a homomorphism with a
772	  % translation" form — which is what the genus-0 consumer needs. The conclusion IsMonHom α
773	  % (one_hom + mul_hom) is a faithful, slightly stronger rendering of "α is a homomorphism".
774	  % As with lem:hom_additivity_over_product, the signature carries the product-level instances
775	  % [GeometricallyIrreducible (A⊗A).hom], [LocallyOfFiniteType (A⊗A).hom], [IsReduced (A⊗A).left]
776	  % ("A×A is a variety"), free for a genuine abelian variety A but carried explicitly because
777	  % Mathlib lacks the product-instance inference. (lean-vs-blueprint-checker avr-iter163.)
778	  % NOTE: (iter-164 review) iter-164 hygiene dropped the B-side instances [Smooth B.hom] and
779	  % [GeometricallyIrreducible B.hom] from the Lean signature; the target B now requires only
780	  % [GrpObj B] [IsProper B.hom]. (The source-side A retains the full abelian-variety instance
781	  % set.) The Lean signature is strictly more general on B than the prose ("α : A → B of
782	  % abelian varieties") demands. The lean-auditor flagged the symmetric A-side
783	  % [Smooth A.hom]/[GeometricallyIrreducible A.hom] as ALSO unused — kept this iter as a
784	  % minimal-change follow-up. (lean-vs-blueprint-checker avr-iter164.)
785	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Corollary 1.2, \S I.1, doc p.~9 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 15)
786	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "COROLLARY 1.2. Every regular map α:A→B of abelian varieties is the composite
787	  % of a homomorphism with a translation."
788	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Corollary~1.2.}
789	  Every regular map $\alpha \colon A \to B$ of abelian varieties over $\bar k$ is the composite
790	  of a homomorphism with a translation; equivalently, if $\alpha(0_A) = 0_B$ then $\alpha$ is a
791	  homomorphism, i.e.\ $\alpha(a + a') = \alpha(a) + \alpha(a')$ for all $a, a' \in A$.
792	\end{lemma}
793	
794	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. The regular map α will send the k-rational point 0 of A to a
795	% k-rational point b of B. After composing α with translation by −b, we may assume that
796	% α(0)=0. Consider the map φ:A×A→B, φ(a,a′)=α(a+a′) − α(a) − α(a′). By this we mean that φ is
797	% the difference of the two regular maps A×A →(m)→ A →(α)→ B and A×A →(α×α)→ B×B →(m)→ B, which
798	% is a regular map. Then φ(A×0)=0=φ(0×A) and so φ=0. This means that α is a homomorphism."
799	% [Milne, Cor 1.2, \S I.1, doc p.~9.]

800	\begin{proof}
801	  \uses{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}
802	  \leanok
803	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Corollary~1.2.}
804	  The image $\alpha(0_A)$ is a $\bar k$-point $b$ of $B$; composing $\alpha$ with translation by
805	  $-b$ we may assume $\alpha(0_A) = 0_B$, and it suffices to show such an $\alpha$ is a
806	  homomorphism. Form
807	  \[ \varphi \colon A \times A \to B, \qquad \varphi(a, a') = \alpha(a + a') - \alpha(a) - \alpha(a'), \]
808	  the difference of the two regular maps $A \times A \xrightarrow{m} A \xrightarrow{\alpha} B$
809	  and $A \times A \xrightarrow{\alpha \times \alpha} B \times B \xrightarrow{m} B$. Then
810	  $\varphi(0_A, 0_A) = 0$ and $\varphi$ vanishes on both axes,
811	  $\varphi(A \times 0) = 0 = \varphi(0 \times A)$ (since $\alpha(0) = 0$). By the additive
812	  decomposition \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} (with $V = W = A$), the axis-restrictions
813	  of $\varphi$ being $0$ force $\varphi \equiv 0$; equivalently, $\varphi$ collapses the complete
814	  axis $A \times 0$ and vanishes on $0 \times A$, so Rigidity gives $\varphi = 0$. Hence
815	  $\alpha(a + a') = \alpha(a) + \alpha(a')$, i.e.\ $\alpha$ is a homomorphism.
816	\end{proof}

844	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Combine Theorem 3.1 with the next lemma." [Milne, Thm 3.2, \S I.3,
845	% doc p.~17.]
846	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF (Thm 3.1, general case): "Let U be the largest subset on which φ is
847	% defined, and suppose that V∖U has codimension 1. Then there is a prime divisor Z in V∖U.

849	% fractions k(V). The map φ defines a morphism of schemes Spec(k(V)) → W, which the valuative
850	% criterion of properness (Hartshorne 1977, II 4.7) shows extends to a morphism Spec(O_Z) → W.
851	% This implies that φ has a representative defined on an open subset that meets Z in a nonempty
852	% set, which is a contradiction." [Milne, Thm 3.1, \S I.3, doc p.~17.]
853	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF (Lemma 3.3, the difference-map): "Define a rational map Φ:V×V ⇢ G,
854	% (x,y) ↦ φ(x)·φ(y)⁻¹. ... Thus φ is defined at x if and only if Φ is defined at (x,x). ...
855	% Suppose φ is not defined at x. Then for some f ∈ Im(Φ*), (x,x) ∈ div(f)₁, and clearly Φ is
856	% not defined at the points (y,y) ∈ Δ ∩ div(f)₁. This is a subset of pure codimension one in Δ,
857	% and when we identify it with a subset of V, it is a subset of V of codimension one passing
858	% through x on which φ is not defined." [Milne, Lemma 3.3, \S I.3, doc p.~17--18.]
859	\begin{proof}
860	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Theorem~3.2.}
861	  The statement is Theorem~3.1 combined with Lemma~3.3. By Theorem~3.1 (a consequence of the
862	  valuative criterion of properness, Mathlib \texttt{IsProper}/\texttt{ValuativeCriterion},
863	  applied at the discrete valuation ring of each codimension-$1$ point), a rational map from the
864	  normal $V$ to the complete $A$ is defined outside a closed set of codimension $\geq 2$. By
865	  Lemma~3.3, the indeterminacy locus of a rational map into a \emph{group} variety is either
866	  empty or pure of codimension $1$: forming the difference map
867	  $\Phi(x, y) = \varphi(x)\,\varphi(y)^{-1} \colon V \times V \dashrightarrow A$, one shows
868	  $\varphi$ is defined at $x$ iff $\Phi$ is defined at the diagonal point $(x, x)$, and an
869	  indeterminacy point produces a pure-codimension-$1$ component of the non-domain. The two
870	  facts are compatible only if the indeterminacy locus is empty; hence $\alpha$ is everywhere
871	  defined.
872	\end{proof}
873	
874	\begin{remark}
875	  \label{rmk:thm32_codim1_mathlib_gap}
876	  \textbf{Formalisation risk: the pure-codimension-$1$ step (Lemma 3.3) has no Mathlib
877	  Weil-divisor support.} Theorem~3.1's everywhere-extension-outside-codimension-$2$ is within
878	  reach of Mathlib's valuative criterion of properness
879	  (\texttt{ValuativeCriterion}/\texttt{IsProper.of\_valuativeCriterion}), applied at the
880	  discrete valuation ring $\mathcal O_{V, Z}$ of each codimension-$1$ point $Z$. But Lemma~3.3,
881	  which rules out codimension-$1$ indeterminacy for maps into a group variety, is proved in
882	  Milne via Weil-divisor theory ($\mathrm{div}(f)_0 - \mathrm{div}(f)_1$, prime divisors,
883	  tangent cones) for which Mathlib currently has \emph{no} usable API at this generality. This
884	  is the route's riskiest sub-build. An open question for the prover: whether a \emph{pointwise}
885	  valuative extension --- showing, at each codimension-$1$ point $Z$ separately, that the
886	  morphism $\Spec k(V) \to A$ extends over $\Spec \mathcal O_{V, Z}$ using only that $A$ is
887	  proper and a group (so the difference-map argument can be run \emph{locally} at $Z$ without
888	  globally building $\mathrm{div}(f)$) --- side-steps building divisor theory entirely.
889	
890	  \textbf{This surface extension is \emph{not} on the genus-$0$ critical path (corrected, iter-164).}
891	  An earlier draft routed the genus-$0$ base case through this extension, by collapsing the
892	  additive-defect map $\psi(x, y) = f(x + y) - f(x) - f(y)$ on the \emph{affine} surface
893	  $\mathbb G_a \times \mathbb G_a$ and then extending $\psi$ over the complete surface
894	  $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1$ --- which genuinely \emph{would} need Theorem~3.2's surface case
895	  (Milne Theorem~3.4 / Lemma~3.3). That detour is unnecessary. The 4th route
896	  (\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}) replaces the two-variable affine defect map by the
897	  \emph{total} translation action $\sigma \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_a \to \mathbb P^1$
898	  (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}): $h := \sigma \fatsemi f$ is a total morphism on $\mathbb P^1
899	  \times \mathbb G_a$ (no extension needed), and the complete-source factor is $\mathbb P^1$
900	  itself, so the additive decomposition \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} applies directly.
901	  Consequently \cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends} (Theorem~3.2), the codim-$1$ indeterminacy
902	  sub-build (Lemma~3.3), and the Weil-divisor gap discussed above are all confined to
903	  \textbf{Route~A} (the positive-genus Albanese universal property, Milne \S III.6); they are
904	  retained in this chapter solely as Route-A inputs and may be deferred until Route~A consumes
905	  them. The genus-$0$ close no longer depends on any of them.
906	\end{remark}

911	[Concrete genus-$0$ base objects: $\mathbb P^1$, $\mathbb G_a$, $\mathbb G_m$ over $\bar k$]
912	  \label{def:genus0_base_objects}
913	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveLineBar}
914	  % Archon-original Lean encoding of standard objects; the mathematics is textbook (Hartshorne
915	  % I.2--3 for the projective line; the group laws on $\mathbb A^1$ are elementary). No external
916	  % % SOURCE QUOTE is needed for the Lean encoding itself.
917	  Work over $\Spec \bar k$ with $\bar k$ algebraically closed. The primary $\mathbb G_m$-scaling
918	  route (and its $\mathbb G_a$-additive alternative) needs only three concrete objects of
919	  $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)$:
920	  \begin{itemize}
921	    \item $\mathbb P^1 = \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$, the projective line --- a smooth proper
922	      geometrically irreducible curve of genus $0$ (Mathlib: projective space, or $\mathrm{Proj}$
923	      of the graded polynomial ring; \lean name \texttt{ProjectiveLineBar}). It carries
924	      the distinguished $\bar k$-points $0$, $1$, $\infty$ and the two standard affine charts
925	      $\mathbb A^1 = \mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}$ (coordinate $x$) and
926	      $\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{0\}$ (coordinate $u = 1/x$).
927	    \item $\mathbb G_a = \mathbb A^1_{\bar k}$, the affine line as the \emph{additive} group object
928	      (Mathlib: \texttt{AffineSpace}\,$\mathbb A(1; \Spec \bar k)$ with the $\mathtt{GrpObj}$
929	      additive structure $(x, y) \mapsto x + y$, identity $0$; \lean name \texttt{Ga}).
930	      As a scheme $\mathbb G_a = \mathbb A^1 = \mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}$, and the open
931	      immersion $\mathbb G_a \hookrightarrow \mathbb P^1$ has dense (cofinite) image.
932	    \item $\mathbb G_m = \mathbb A^1 \setminus \{0\} = \mathbb P^1 \setminus \{0, \infty\}$, the
933	      \emph{multiplicative} group object (group law $(x, y) \mapsto xy$, identity $1$; \lean name
934	      \texttt{Gm}). Its open immersion into $\mathbb P^1$ also has dense image.
935	  \end{itemize}
936	  All three are of finite type over $\bar k$; $\mathbb P^1$ is proper, while $\mathbb G_a$ and
937	  $\mathbb G_m$ are affine (hence not complete). These are the only ``genus-$0$'' geometric inputs
938	  the base case needs.
939	\end{definition}

1035	\subsection*{Chart cover and chart-ring iso (formalisation infrastructure)}
1036	
1037	The next three declarations are iter-168 formalisation infrastructure that the chartwise
1038	construction of the $\mathbb G_m$-scaling action $\sigma_\times$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1})
1039	and its downstream consumers (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}) ultimately rest on.
1040	They realise, at the Lean level, the standard ``affine chart of $\mathbb P^1$ is $\mathbb
1041	A^1$'' picture: a two-chart affine open cover of $\overline{\mathbb P^1}$ by $D_+(X_0)$ and
1042	$D_+(X_1)$; the identification of each chart ring with the one-variable polynomial ring
1043	$\bar k[u]$; and the resulting reducedness of $\overline{\mathbb P^1}$ as a scheme. None
1044	of them is on the genus-$0$ critical path conceptually, but each is consumed by the Lean
1045	formalisation downstream.
1046	
1047	\begin{definition}
1048	[Two-chart affine open cover of $\overline{\mathbb P^1}$]
1049	  \label{def:projlinebar_affine_cover}
1050	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.projectiveLineBarAffineCover}
1051	  \uses{def:genus0_base_objects}

1053	  % two standard affine charts $D_+(X_0)$ and $D_+(X_1)$; the construction is built on top of
1054	  % Mathlib's $\mathrm{Proj.affineOpenCoverOfIrrelevantLESpan}$. No external % SOURCE QUOTE
1055	  % needed for the Lean encoding itself.
1056	  The two-chart affine open cover of $\overline{\mathbb P^1}$ by the standard $D_+(X_0)$ and
1057	  $D_+(X_1)$, built by specialising Mathlib's
1058	  $\mathrm{Proj.affineOpenCoverOfIrrelevantLESpan}$ to the index set
1059	  $\iota = \{0, 1\} = \mathrm{Fin}\,2$ with family $f = (X_0, X_1)$ and weights
1060	  $m = (1, 1)$. The non-trivial input is that the irrelevant ideal of the bi-graded
1061	  $\bar k[X_0, X_1]$ is contained in $\mathrm{Ideal.span}\{X_0, X_1\}$, which holds by
1062	  writing any irrelevant element as a sum of monomials of strictly positive total degree
1063	  (so each monomial is divisible by $X_0$ or $X_1$). This cover packages the two affine
1064	  charts into the data that Mathlib's $\mathrm{IsReduced.of\_openCover}$ and
1065	  $\mathrm{Scheme.Cover.glueMorphisms}$ APIs consume, and is the chartwise glue for the

1072	  \label{def:proj_chart_ring_iso}

1079	  degree-zero part $\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i$ of the localization of the standard
1080	  $\mathbb N$-graded polynomial ring $\bar k[X_0, X_1]$ at $X_i$ --- is isomorphic as a
1081	  $\bar k$-algebra to the one-variable polynomial ring $\bar k[u]$, where the affine
1082	  chart coordinate is $u = X_{1-i}/X_i$. This is the formalisation-level realisation of

1351	  \[ \sigma_\times \fatsemi f = \mathrm{pr}_1 \fatsemi f \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to A,
1352	     \qquad\text{``}f(\lambda x) = f(x)\text{''}. \]
1353	  Precomposing with $\lambda \mapsto (1, \lambda)$ gives $f|_{\mathbb G_m} = \text{const}\,f(1)$;

1364	  \noindent\textbf{Retained alternative --- the $\mathbb G_a$-additive route.} The Milne-faithful
1365	  argument routes through the translation action $\sigma \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_a \to

1370	  needs the triviality lemma; it is retained for fidelity to Milne and is \emph{off} the genus-$0$

1374	\begin{lemma}
1375	[A pointed $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant (Milne Prop 3.9, via the 4th route)]
1376	  \label{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}
1377	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.morphism_Ga_to_av_const}
1378	  \uses{thm:rigidity_lemma, lem:hom_additivity_over_product, def:gaTranslationP1, lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}
1379	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition 3.9, \S I.3, doc p.~19--20 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF pages 25--26)
1380	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "PROPOSITION 3.9. Every rational map A¹ ⇢ A or P¹ ⇢ A is constant."
1381	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.9.}
1382	
1383	  \textbf{Off the genus-$0$ critical path (iter-164): retained alternative route.} This
1384	  $\mathbb G_a$-additive close of the base case is \emph{superseded by the $\mathbb G_m$-scaling
1385	  shortcut} now installed as the proof of \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} (see
1386	  \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). It is retained as the classical Milne Proposition~3.9
1387	  argument (faithful to the $\mathbb G_a \to A$ homomorphism / $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$
1388	  presentation), but the live consumer \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} no longer depends on
1389	  it; it is provided here only for completeness.
1390	
1391	  Let $A$ be an abelian variety over $\bar k$ and $f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ a
1392	  morphism with $f(0) = 0_A$ (the additive-identity $\bar k$-point of $\mathbb G_a = \mathbb A^1
1393	  \subset \mathbb P^1$; any base point reduces to this by a normalising translation in $A$).
1394	  Then $f$ is constant. Concretely: the translation action $\sigma$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1})
1395	  makes $f|_{\mathbb G_a}$ an additive-group homomorphism $\mathbb G_a \to A$, and a homomorphism
1396	  $\mathbb G_a \to A$ is trivial (\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}), so $f$ collapses to the
1397	  constant $0_A$.
1398	\end{lemma}

1400	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. According to (3.2), α extends to a regular map on the whole of A¹.
1401	% After composing α with a translation, we may suppose that α(0)=0. Then α is a homomorphism,
1402	% α(x+y)=α(x)+α(y); all x,y ∈ A¹(k^al)=k^al. But A¹−{0} is also a group variety, and similarly,
1403	% α(xy)=α(x)+α(y)+c; all x,y ∈ A¹(k^al)=k^al. This is absurd, unless α is constant." [Milne,
1404	% Prop 3.9, \S I.3, doc p.~19--20.]
1405	\begin{proof}
1406	  \uses{thm:rigidity_lemma, lem:hom_additivity_over_product, def:gaTranslationP1, lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}
1407	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.9.}
1408	  Normalise $f(0) = 0_A$ by a translation in $A$ (un-translate at the end). The proof is the
1409	  \emph{4th route} (\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}): the additive defect map is never built on
1410	  the affine surface and never extended; instead we precompose $f$ with the \emph{total}
1411	  translation action $\sigma$, and the complete factor is $\mathbb P^1$ itself, so the proven
1412	  additive decomposition applies directly. Milne's Theorem~3.2
1413	  (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) is \emph{not} used.
1414	
1415	  \textbf{$f|_{\mathbb G_a}$ is an additive homomorphism, via the total action $\sigma$.} Form
1416	  \[ h := \sigma \fatsemi f \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_a \to A, \qquad
1417	     \text{``}h(x, y) = f(x + y)\text{''}, \]
1418	  using the translation action $\sigma$ of \cref{def:gaTranslationP1}; this is a \emph{total}
1419	  morphism (no rational-map extension needed). Apply the additive decomposition
1420	  \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} (Milne Cor~1.5) --- whose Lean signature requires only the
1421	  \emph{first} factor complete --- with $V = \mathbb P^1$ (proper), $W = \mathbb G_a$, and base
1422	  points $0 \in \mathbb P^1$, $0 \in \mathbb G_a$, noting $h(0, 0) = f(0) = 0_A$. The axis
1423	  restrictions are $x \mapsto h(x, 0) = f(x)$ and $y \mapsto h(0, y) = f(y)$, so the decomposition
1424	  $h = (p \fatsemi f) + (q \fatsemi (f|_{\mathbb G_a}))$ reads, on $\bar k$-points,
1425	  \[ f(x + y) = f(x) + f(y), \qquad x \in \mathbb P^1(\bar k),\ y \in \mathbb G_a(\bar k) = \bar k. \]
1426	  Restricting $x$ to $\mathbb G_a$ shows $f|_{\mathbb G_a} \colon \mathbb G_a \to A$ is a
1427	  homomorphism of group objects (it sends $0 \mapsto 0_A$ and respects addition).
1428	
1429	  \textbf{A homomorphism $\mathbb G_a \to A$ is trivial.} By \cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}
1430	  (the $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$ lemma, the content of Milne's $\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m$
1431	  incompatibility), $f|_{\mathbb G_a}$ is the constant morphism at $0_A$. Hence $f$ is constant on
1432	  the dense open $\mathbb G_a \subset \mathbb P^1$; since $\mathbb G_a$ is dense and $A$ is
1433	  separated, $f$ is constant on all of $\mathbb P^1$, with value $f(0) = 0_A$ (i.e.\ the original
1434	  base value before the normalising translation). \qedhere
1435	
1436	  \medskip
1437	  \noindent\emph{Note: this route is the demoted alternative.} The primary base-case proof is the
1438	  $\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut now installed directly as the proof of
1439	  \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} (\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}): the multiplicative
1440	  scaling action $\sigma_\times$ of \cref{def:gaTranslationP1} gives $\sigma_\times \fatsemi f =
1441	  \mathrm{pr}_1 \fatsemi f$ (the $W$-axis collapses because $0$ is a scaling fixed point), so
1442	  $f|_{\mathbb G_m}$ is constant and $f$ is constant by density --- bypassing both the $\mathbb
1443	  G_a$-additivity step and \cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}, and avoiding the ``image is a closed
1444	  subgroup'' Mathlib gap. The present $\mathbb G_a$-additive lemma is retained only for fidelity to
1445	  Milne; it is not on the genus-$0$ critical path.
1446	\end{proof}

1448	\section{Morphisms from $\mathbb P^1$ to an abelian variety are constant}
1449	
1450	\begin{proposition}
1451	
1452	\leanok
1453	[A morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant]
1454	  \label{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}
1455	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const}
1456	  \uses{lem:hom_additivity_over_product, def:gaTranslationP1, lem:gmScaling_fixes_zero, thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}
1457	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition 3.10 (specialised to a single $\mathbb P^1$ factor), \S I.3, doc p.~20 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 26)
1458	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "PROPOSITION 3.10. Every rational map $\alpha \colon V \dashrightarrow A$
1459	  % from a unirational variety to an abelian variety is constant."
1460	  % SOURCE QUOTE (unirational, same page): "A variety $V$ over an algebraically closed field
1461	  % is said to be unirational if there is a dominating rational map $\mathbb A^n
1462	  % \dashrightarrow V$ with $n = \dim V$; equivalently, if $k(V)$ can be embedded into
1463	  % $k(X_1, \ldots, X_n)$ (pure transcendental extension of $k$). A variety $V$ over an
1464	  % arbitrary field $k$ is said to be unirational if $V_{k^{al}}$ is unirational."
1465	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.10 (case $V = \mathbb P^1$).}
1466	  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and $A$ an abelian variety over $\bar k$. Then every
1467	  morphism $f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is constant: $f(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k})$ is a
1468	  single $\bar k$-point.
1469	\end{proposition}
1470	
1471	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. We may suppose that $k$ is algebraically closed. By assumption
1472	% there is a rational map $\mathbb A^n \dashrightarrow V$ with dense image, and the composite
1473	% of this with $\alpha$ extends to a morphism $\beta \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \cdots \times
1474	% \mathbb P^1 \to A$. An induction argument, starting from Corollary 1.5, shows that there are
1475	% regular maps $\beta_i \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ such that $\beta(x_1, \ldots, x_d) = \sum
1476	% \beta_i(x_i)$, and the lemma shows that each $\beta_i$ is constant."
1477	\begin{proof}
1478	  \uses{lem:hom_additivity_over_product, def:gaTranslationP1, lem:gmScaling_fixes_zero, thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}
1479	  \leanok
1480	  This is the single-factor ($d = 1$) instance of Milne's Proposition~3.10, where the
1481	  unirational variety is $\mathbb P^1$ itself. We give the project's \textbf{primary} proof, the
1482	  \emph{$\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut}: it proves constancy \emph{in one shot} from the proven
1483	  additive decomposition (\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}) and a scaling fixed point,
1484	  without the intermediate ``$f|_{\mathbb G_a}$ additive'' step and \emph{without} the
1485	  $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$ triviality lemma. The theorem of the cube is \emph{not} used
1486	  (\cref{rmk:cube_not_needed}), and the map $f$ is already a morphism out of the complete
1487	  nonsingular $\mathbb P^1$, so Milne's Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) is
1488	  \emph{not} invoked either.
1489	
1490	  \textbf{Reduction to a base point.} Choose the scaling fixed point $0 \in \mathbb G_a \subset
1491	  \mathbb P^1$ (one of the two fixed points of $\sigma_\times$). Translating in the group $A$ by
1492	  $-f(0)$, we may assume $f(0) = 0_A$; un-translating at the end restores the original value.
1493	
1494	  \textbf{The scaling shortcut.} Form the \emph{total} morphism
1495	  \[ h := \sigma_\times \fatsemi f \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to A, \qquad
1496	     \text{``}h(x, \lambda) = f(\lambda x)\text{''}, \]
1497	  using the multiplicative scaling action $\sigma_\times$ of \cref{def:gaTranslationP1} (a genuine
1498	  total morphism --- no rational-map extension needed). Apply the additive decomposition
1499	  \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} (Milne Cor~1.5) --- whose Lean signature requires only the
1500	  \emph{first} factor complete --- with $V = \mathbb P^1$ (proper), $W = \mathbb G_m$, and base
1501	  points $0 \in \mathbb P^1$, $1 \in \mathbb G_m$; here $h(0, 1) = f(1 \cdot 0) = f(0) = 0_A$, as
1502	  required. The decomposition writes $h = (p \fatsemi f_0) + (q \fatsemi g)$ with $f_0$ the
1503	  $V$-axis restriction $x \mapsto h(x, 1) = f(1 \cdot x) = f(x)$ (so $f_0 = f$) and $g$ the
1504	  $W$-axis restriction $\lambda \mapsto h(0, \lambda) = f(\lambda \cdot 0) = f(0) = 0_A$.
1505	
1506	  \textbf{The $W$-axis collapses (the load-bearing scaling fixed point).} Because $0$ is a
1507	  \emph{fixed point} of scaling, $\sigma_\times(0, \lambda) = 0$ for all $\lambda$
1508	  (\cref{lem:gmScaling_fixes_zero}), so the $W$-axis
1509	  restriction $g$ is the \emph{constant} morphism at $0_A$ --- the identity of the hom-group
1510	  $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_m, A)$. The corresponding summand $q \fatsemi g$ is therefore the group
1511	  identity, and the decomposition collapses to
1512	  \[ h = p \fatsemi f, \qquad\text{i.e.}\qquad
1513	     \sigma_\times \fatsemi f = \mathrm{pr}_1 \fatsemi f, \qquad
1514	     \text{``}f(\lambda x) = f(x)\text{''} \quad (x \in \mathbb P^1,\ \lambda \in \mathbb G_m). \]
1515	
1516	  \textbf{Constancy via density.} Specialise $x = 1$ (precompose with $\lambda \mapsto (1,
1517	  \lambda)$): $f(\lambda) = f(1)$ for all $\lambda \in \mathbb G_m$, i.e.\ $f|_{\mathbb G_m}$ is
1518	  the constant morphism at $f(1)$. Since $\mathbb G_m$ is dense in the irreducible $\mathbb P^1$
1519	  and $A$ is separated, the two morphisms $f$ and $\mathrm{const}_{f(1)}$, agreeing on the
1520	  non-empty open $\mathbb G_m$, agree everywhere by the dominant-source/separated-target rigidity
1521	  handle \cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} (the same \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant} family
1522	  \texttt{rigidity\_core} uses). Hence $f$ is constant, with value $f(1)$; in the normalised
1523	  picture $f(0) = 0_A$, so the constant is $0_A$ (the original $f(0)$ before translation).
1524	
1525	  \medskip
1526	  \noindent\emph{Multi-factor remark.} For the general unirational statement of Proposition~3.10,
1527	  one writes $\beta(x_1, \ldots, x_d) = \sum_i \beta_i(x_i)$ via the additive decomposition
1528	  (\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}) and applies this base case to each $\beta_i \colon
1529	  \mathbb P^1 \to A$; we need only the $d = 1$ case here. The Milne-faithful $\mathbb
1530	  G_a$-additive route (\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}, via $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$,
1531	  \cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}) reaches the same conclusion and is retained as an alternative,
1532	  but is \emph{not} on this critical path.
1533

1578	\section{A genus-$0$ curve over $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$}
1579	
1580	\begin{proposition}
1581	
1582	\leanok
1583	[Genus-$0$ curve $\cong \mathbb P^1$ over $\bar k$]
1584	  \label{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
1585	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
1586	  \uses{def:genus}
1587	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne, Algebraic Geometry], Example IV.1.3.5, Ch.~IV \S1 (Riemann--Roch), doc p.~297 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314)
1588	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Example 1.3.5. Recall that a curve is rational if it is birational to
1589	  % $\mathbf P^1$ (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in this chapter are complete and nonsingular by
1590	  % definition, a curve $X$ is rational if and only if $X \cong \mathbf P^1$ (I, 6.12). Now
1591	  % using (1.3) we can show that $X$ is rational if and only if $g = 0$. We already know that
1592	  % $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ (I, Ex. 7.2), so conversely suppose given a curve $X$ of genus 0.
1593	  % Let $P, Q$ be two distinct points of $X$ and apply Riemann-Roch to the divisor
1594	  % $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg(K - D) = -2$, using (1.3.3) above, we have $l(K - D) = 0$, and so
1595	  % we find $l(D) = 1$. But $D$ is a divisor of degree 0, so by (1.2) we have $D \sim 0$, in
1596	  % other words $P \sim Q$. But this implies $X$ is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
1597	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, Algebraic Geometry, Example~IV.1.3.5.}
1598	  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and $C$ a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
1599	  over $\bar k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$. Then $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
1600	\end{proposition}
1601	
1602	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the verbatim Example IV.1.3.5 text in the statement block above
1603	% (Hartshorne states the classification and its proof inside the single Example; the proof is
1604	% the second half beginning ``We already know that $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ \ldots'').
1605	\begin{proof}
1606	  \uses{def:genus}
1607	  \emph{Proof sketch (Riemann--Roch level; the formalisation of this step is itself a
1608	  sub-build --- Mathlib has no Riemann--Roch for curves).}
1609	
1610	  Over the algebraically closed field $\bar k$ every closed point is $\bar k$-rational, so $C$
1611	  has at least two distinct $\bar k$-points $P \neq Q$ (a smooth proper geometrically
1612	  irreducible curve over $\bar k$ is infinite). With $\genus(C) = \dim_{\bar k}
1613	  H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$, apply the Riemann--Roch theorem to the degree-$0$ divisor
1614	  $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg(K - D) = 2g - 2 - \deg D = -2 < 0$ one has $l(K - D) = 0$, so
1615	  Riemann--Roch $l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g$ gives $l(D) = 0 + 1 - 0 = 1$. Thus the
1616	  degree-$0$ divisor $D$ is linearly equivalent to an effective divisor, forcing $D \sim 0$,
1617	  i.e.\ $P \sim Q$. A curve possessing two distinct linearly equivalent points is rational
1618	  (Hartshorne II.6.10.1: $P \sim Q$ for $P \neq Q$ yields a degree-$1$ map to $\mathbb P^1$),
1619	  and a complete nonsingular rational curve is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$ (Hartshorne I.6.12).
1620	  Hence $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
1621	\end{proof}
1622	
1623	\begin{remark}
1624	  \label{rmk:genusZero_iso_subbuild}
1625	  \cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} is Riemann--Roch--flavoured and its formalisation is a
1626	  genuine sub-build, not a short step: Mathlib has no Riemann--Roch theorem for curves, no
1627	  divisor class group machinery at this level, and no $\mathbb P^1$-classification of
1628	  rational curves. The statement is stated here at textbook level so the chain is complete;
1629	  closing its Lean proof will require either importing/porting a substantial slice of curve
1630	  theory or re-deriving the genus-$0 \Rightarrow \mathbb P^1$ classification from the
1631	  project's own $\genus$ definition. The dependency on $\genus$ (\cref{def:genus}) is the link
1632	  to \cref{chap:Genus}.
1633	\end{remark}
1634	
1635	\section{The headline: rigidity for a pointed genus-$0$ curve}
1636	
1637	\begin{theorem}
1638	
1639	\leanok
1640	[Rigidity: a pointed morphism from a genus-$0$ curve to an abelian variety is constant]
1641	  \label{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}
1642	  \label{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}
1643	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme}
1644	  \uses{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant, prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
1645	  Let $\bar k$ be an algebraically closed field (\emph{arbitrary characteristic}; no
1646	  $\mathtt{CharZero}$ hypothesis). Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
1647	  over $\bar k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$, and let $A$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
1648	  group scheme over $\bar k$ (an abelian variety) with identity $\eta_A$. Then for every
1649	  morphism $f \colon C \to A$ such that $p \fatsemi f = \eta_A$ for some $\bar k$-point
1650	  $p \colon \mathbf 1 \to C$, the morphism $f$ equals the constant morphism at the identity,
1651	  $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A$.
1652	
1653	  This is the project's committed characteristic-free statement that the genus-$0$
1654	  Albanese-witness $\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}$ consumes. Its signature mirrors
1655	  $\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar}$ of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}
1656	  \emph{verbatim except} that the $[\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]$ instance is dropped: same
1657	  curve typeclasses ($\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\ 1$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$,
1658	  $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$ on $C$), same abelian-variety typeclasses
1659	  ($\mathtt{GrpObj}$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$, $\mathtt{Smooth}$, $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$
1660	  on $A$), and the same pointed-morphism conclusion shape $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi
1661	  \eta[A]$.
1662	\end{theorem}
1663	
1664	\begin{proof}
1665	  \uses{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant, prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
1666	  \leanok
1667	  By \cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} the genus-$0$ curve $C$ over the algebraically closed
1668	  field $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ via some isomorphism
1669	  $\varphi \colon C \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$. The composite
1670	  $f \circ \varphi^{-1} \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is then a morphism from
1671	  $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ to the abelian variety $A$, hence constant by
1672	  \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}: its image is a single $\bar k$-point $a_0 \in A$.
1673	  Therefore $f$ itself is constant with value $a_0$. The pointed hypothesis
1674	  $p \fatsemi f = \eta_A$ evaluates this constant at the $\bar k$-point $p$ and pins it to the
1675	  identity, $a_0 = \eta_A$. Consequently $f$ factors through the terminal object as the
1676	  constant morphism at the identity, $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A$.
1677	
1678	  No differential ($df = 0$) input, no $H^0(C, \Omega_C) = 0$ / Serre-duality decomposition,
1679	  and no Picard representability is used; the argument is valid in arbitrary characteristic.
1680	  Once formalised it lets the $[\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]$ hypothesis carried by
1681	  $\mathtt{rigidity\_over\_kbar}$ be dropped, and (after the $\bar k \to k$ descent step hosted
1682	  in \cref{chap:Jacobian}, not here) feeds $\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}.\mathtt{key}$.
1683	
1684	  % NOTE (iter-166 review): proof body landed iter-166 via clean iso-transport
1685	  % (`obtain ⟨φ⟩ := genusZero_curve_iso_P1 _hgenus; set g := φ.inv ≫ f; obtain ⟨a₀, hga₀⟩ :=
1686	  % morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const g; pin a₀ = η[A] via `toUnit_unique` basepoint transport;
1687	  % back-transport via `φ.hom_inv_id`'). `lean_verify` axioms $\{\mathtt{propext},
1688	  % \mathtt{sorryAx}, \mathtt{Classical.choice}, \mathtt{Quot.sound}\}$ — $\mathtt{sorryAx}$
1689	  % propagates honestly through (a) `genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (RR-bridge sub-build, deferred to
1690	  % iter-167+) and (b) `morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const`'s 5 helper sorries. Lifts to axiom-clean
1691	  % once both upstreams discharge; checker confirmed no laundering.
1692	\end{proof}

201	\begin{theorem}\leanok
202	[Geometric irreducibility]
203	  \label{thm:Jacobian_geomIrred}
204	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.instGeometricallyIrreducible}
205	  \uses{def:Jacobian}
206	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman, The Picard scheme], Lem.~lem:agps (the identity component $G^0$ is open-closed, finite type, geometrically irreducible, commutes with field extension) + Prp.~prp:pic0 (specialisation to $\IPicz_{X/k}$); arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~30, 31  (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex)
207	  % SOURCE QUOTE (lem:agps): "Let $k$ be a field, and $G$ a group scheme
208	  % locally of finite type.  Let $G^0$ denote the connected component of the
209	  % identity element $e$.  \tu{(1)} Then $G$ is separated.  \tu{(2)} Then $G$
210	  % is smooth if it has a geometrically reduced open subscheme.  \tu{(3)}
211	  % Then $G^0$ is an open and closed group subscheme of finite type; it is
212	  % geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with extending $k$."
213	  % SOURCE QUOTE (prp:pic0): "Assume $S$ is the spectrum of a field $k$.
214	  % Assume $\IPic_{X/k}$ exists and represents $\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}$.  Then
215	  % $\IPic_{X/k}$ is separated, and it is smooth if it has a geometrically
216	  % reduced open subscheme.  Furthermore, the connected component of the
217	  % identity $\IPicz_{X/k}$ is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
218	  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
219	  % extending $k$."
220	  \textit{Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Lem.~(lem:agps), Prp.~(prp:pic0).}
221	  $\Jac(C) \to \Spec k$ is geometrically irreducible.
222	\end{theorem}
223	
224	\begin{proof}
225	  \uses{def:Jacobian, thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}
226	  The Lean formalisation projects the field $\mathtt{geomIrred} : \mathrm{GeometricallyIrreducible}\, J.\mathtt{hom}$ of the witness.
227	
228	  Mathematically, the Albanese variety $\Jac(C)$ is the identity component of $\Pic_{C/k}$ (the Picard scheme), and the identity component of a group scheme of finite type over a field is geometrically irreducible (in fact, geometrically connected, and since it is smooth at the identity by Theorem~\ref{thm:Jacobian_smooth_genus} it is geometrically irreducible). Equivalently, in the symmetric-power route, $\Jac(C)$ arises as the Stein factorisation of $\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}$ followed by a translation; $\Sym^g(C)$ is geometrically irreducible (being the quotient of $C^g$, itself geometrically irreducible as a product of geometrically irreducible factors, by a finite group action), so its image under the Stein factorisation is geometrically irreducible as well.
229	\end{proof}
230	
231	\subsection{Sanity check in low genus}
232	
233	\begin{itemize}
234	  \item $g = 0$: Then $\Jac(C) = \Spec k$, the trivial group scheme; $\Jac(\mathbb P^1_k) = \Spec k$ is smooth of relative dimension $0 = g$.
235	  \item $g = 1$: Then $\Jac(C) \cong C$ once a $k$-rational point on $C$ is fixed; $C$ is itself an elliptic curve (abelian variety of dimension $1$).
236	  \item $g \ge 2$: $\Jac(C)$ is a non-trivial $g$-dimensional abelian variety, never isomorphic to $C$ as a scheme.
237	\end{itemize}
238	
239	\section{Existence of an Albanese variety}
240	
241	The classical construction of the Jacobian goes through the Picard scheme machinery: $\Jac(C)$ is the connected component of the identity of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$. An equivalent route, available whenever a $k$-rational point $P \in C(k)$ has been fixed, is to take the Stein factorisation of the Abel--Jacobi morphism $\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}$ and translate by $-gP$.
242	
243	\subsection{The Albanese witness bundle}
244	
245	Before stating the existence theorem, we package its conclusion into a single structure --- the \emph{Albanese witness} of $C$. This bundling reverses the quantifier order of the classical statement (see Remark~\ref{rem:JacobianWitness_quantifier_order}) and is the data structure consumed by the \texttt{Jacobian} family of declarations in this file and by the \texttt{AbelJacobi} interface of Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi}.
246	
247	\begin{definition}
248	
249	\leanok
250	[Albanese witness for a curve]
251	  \label{def:JacobianWitness}
252	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.JacobianWitness}
253	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese, def:genus}
254	  An \emph{Albanese witness} for the smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C$ over $k$ is a bundle of the following seven fields:
255	  \begin{itemize}
256	    \item $J$: an object of the category of $k$-schemes over $\Spec k$ --- the candidate Albanese object.
257	    \item $\mathtt{grpObj}$: a group-object structure on $J$ in the category of $k$-schemes over $\Spec k$.
258	    \item $\mathtt{proper}$: a proof that the structure morphism $J \to \Spec k$ is proper.
259	    \item $\mathtt{smooth}$: a proof that the structure morphism $J \to \Spec k$ is smooth.
260	    \item $\mathtt{geomIrred}$: a proof that the structure morphism $J \to \Spec k$ is geometrically irreducible.
261	    \item $\mathtt{smoothGenus}$: a proof that the structure morphism $J \to \Spec k$ is smooth of relative dimension $g(C)$. This refines $\mathtt{smooth}$ at the specific relative dimension $\genus\, C$ (see Remark~\ref{rem:JacobianWitness_smooth_redundancy}).
262	    \item $\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}$: a proof that, for every $k$-rational marked point $P \in C(k)$, the scheme $J$ together with its group-object, proper, smooth, and geometrically-irreducible structures satisfies the Albanese universal property for the pointed curve $(C, P)$ in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese}.
263	  \end{itemize}
264	  Formally, an Albanese witness is a Lean-level record (a structure) whose name is \texttt{JacobianWitness}.
265	\end{definition}
266	
267	\begin{remark}
268	  \label{rem:JacobianWitness_quantifier_order}
269	  \uses{def:JacobianWitness}
270	  The classical phrasing of the existence statement of an Albanese variety is
271	  \[
272	    \forall\, P \in C(k),\ \exists\, J,\ J \text{ is an Albanese object for } (C, P).
273	  \]
274	  The witness bundle reverses these quantifiers: a single $J$ is chosen once (intrinsic to $C$), and the marked-point-dependent data is recorded as the family-valued field
275	  \[
276	    \mathtt{isAlbaneseFor} \colon \forall\, P \in C(k),\ \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\,C\,P\,J.
277	  \]
278	  Mathematically this reversal is justified because the underlying scheme of any Albanese object is intrinsic to $C$: a different choice of $P \in C(k)$ alters the universal morphism $\iota_P \colon C \to J$ by a translation of $J$, which is an automorphism of $J$, and so leaves $J$ unchanged as a scheme. The reversed bundling is what makes the per-$P$ projection in Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi} clean: $\mathtt{Jacobian.ofCurve}$, $\mathtt{Jacobian.comp\_ofCurve}$, and $\mathtt{Jacobian.exists\_unique\_ofCurve\_comp}$ each take a marked point $P$ as input and project the corresponding component of $\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}\,P$ via the extraction trio (Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}, Lemmas~\ref{lem:IsAlbanese_comp_ofCurve}, \ref{lem:IsAlbanese_exists_unique_ofCurve_comp}).
279	\end{remark}
280	
281	\begin{remark}
282	  \label{rem:JacobianWitness_smooth_redundancy}
283	  \uses{def:JacobianWitness}
284	  The fields $\mathtt{smooth}$ and $\mathtt{smoothGenus}$ of an Albanese witness are mathematically redundant: the relative-dimension version implies the unqualified version via Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.SmoothOfRelativeDimension.smooth}. Keeping both fields is a Lean-level convenience: $\mathtt{smooth}$ is the form required by the typeclass binder of \texttt{IsAlbanese} (and by the protected $\mathtt{Jacobian.instGrpObj}$ projection), whereas $\mathtt{smoothGenus}$ is the form required by the protected $\mathtt{Jacobian.smoothOfRelativeDimension\_genus}$ projection. The redundancy carries no mathematical cost (the two fields agree) and no soundness cost (deriving one from the other would only re-run the same Mathlib instance synthesis at the projection sites).
285	\end{remark}
286	
287	\begin{theorem}\leanok
288	[Existence of an Albanese variety uniformly over the marked point]
289	  \label{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}
290	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.nonempty_jacobianWitness}
291	  % Cycle-fix (iter-149 plan-agent): drop the spurious \uses{def:Jacobian}
292	  % from this statement-block. Mathematically `def:Jacobian` is the entity
293	  % that depends on this existence theorem (def:Jacobian's \uses{...} lists
294	  % thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness), not the other way around. Listing
295	  % def:Jacobian here as a hypothesis of the existence theorem introduced
296	  % a 2-cycle in plastex's depgraph (def:Jacobian ↔ thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness)
297	  % that crashes `leanblueprint web` with RecursionError in depgraph.ancestors.
298	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese, def:genus, def:JacobianWitness}
299	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman, The Picard scheme], Exercise ex:jac (generalized Jacobians of a family of curves form a smooth quasi-projective family of relative dimension $p_a$, projective iff $X/S$ smooth); arXiv:math/0504020, p.~50  (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex)
300	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Assume $X/S$ is projective and flat, its geometric fibers
301	  % are integral curves of arithmetic genus $p_a$, and $S$ is Noetherian.
302	  % Show the ``generalized Jacobians'' $\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}$ form a smooth
303	  % quasi-projective family of relative dimension $p_a$.  And show this
304	  % family is projective if and only if $X/S$ is smooth."
305	  \textit{Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Exercise (ex:jac).}
306	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over a field $k$. There exists a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme $J$ over $k$ of relative dimension $g(C)$ such that, for every $k$-rational point $P \in C(k)$, the scheme $J$ is an Albanese object for the pointed curve $(C, P)$ in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese}. Equivalently, the type of Albanese witnesses for $C$ (Definition~\ref{def:JacobianWitness}) is non-empty.
307	\end{theorem}
308	
309	\begin{proof}
310	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese, def:genus, thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen, def:genusZeroWitness, thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}
311	  The proof is split into three classical constructions plus the genus-$0$ sub-case absorbed into the same witness (the latter packaged as the explicit construction \cref{def:genusZeroWitness}, which routes through the characteristic-free keystone \cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} of \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} --- rigidity proved over the algebraic closure $\bar k = \mathrm{AlgebraicClosure}\,k$ --- and then \emph{descends} the resulting morphism equality from $\bar k$ to the base field $k$ along the faithfully-flat surjection $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$). Each route produces the same intrinsic scheme $J$; only the universal morphism $\iota_P \colon C \to J$ varies with the marked point $P$. We outline each route and name the Mathlib infrastructure each one requires.
312	
313	  \paragraph{(0) Reduction to a $P$-independent witness.}
314	  The witness $J$ depends only on $C$, not on the marked point $P$. Indeed, both classical constructions below produce $J$ as an intrinsic object of $C$ (the identity component of $\Pic_{C/k}$, or the Stein-factorisation target of $\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}$ translated by a fixed $P_0$); a different choice of $P$ in $C(k)$ alters $\iota_P$ by a translation of $J$, which is an automorphism, and so does not change $J$ as a scheme. The Lean encoding reflects this by bundling $J$ as a single field and the universal-property data as $\forall P,\, \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\, C\, P\, J$. We now turn to producing $J$.
315	
316	  \paragraph{Route A --- Picard scheme.}
317	  This route follows Hartshorne~III.4 and FGA Explained, Chapter~9.
318	
319	  \begin{itemize}
320	    \item[\textbf{(A.1)}] \emph{The relative Picard functor.} For a $k$-scheme $T$, write $C_T := C \times_k T$ and let $\pi \colon C_T \to T$ be the projection. The relative Picard functor is
321	    \[
322	      \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k} \colon (\Sch/k)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \Sets, \qquad
323	      T \longmapsto \Pic(C_T) / \pi^*\Pic(T).
324	    \]
325	    A $T$-point is a line bundle on $C_T$ modulo a line bundle pulled back from $T$. The set-valued formulation suffices for representability; the canonical abelian-group structure on $\Pic(C_T)$ descends to the quotient by $\pi^*\Pic(T)$ and gives $\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}$ a natural group-functor refinement.
326	
327	    \item[\textbf{(A.2)}] \emph{Representability.} For $C$ smooth proper geometrically connected over $k$ with a $k$-rational point, $\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}$ (after \'etale sheafification, if $k$ is not algebraically closed) is representable by a $k$-group scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$, locally of finite type. This is FGA's main representability theorem (Grothendieck, FGA~232); the smooth-proper-geometrically-connected hypothesis ensures that the cohomology-and-base-change machinery applies and that $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = k$, which is needed for the quotient by $\pi^*\Pic(T)$ to be \'etale-locally injective.
328	
329	    \item[\textbf{(A.3)}] \emph{The identity component.} Let $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ be the identity component of $\Pic_{C/k}$. As the identity component of a $k$-group scheme locally of finite type, $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ is geometrically irreducible; for $C$ smooth proper of genus $g$, it is moreover a smooth proper $k$-group scheme of dimension $g$. Smoothness comes from the deformation-theoretic identification of its tangent space at the identity with $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$ and homogeneity of the group action; properness from the fact that the natural map $\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}$ is an open and closed immersion onto a proper component (a consequence of the cohomological criterion: $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ is the kernel of the degree map $\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb Z}_k$, which is locally constant). Take $J := \Pic^0_{C/k}$.
330	
331	    \item[\textbf{(A.4)}] \emph{The Abel--Jacobi universal morphism.} For each $P \in C(k)$, the Abel--Jacobi morphism
332	    \[
333	      \iota_P \colon C \to \Pic^0_{C/k}, \qquad
334	      Q \longmapsto [\mathcal O_C(Q - P)]
335	    \]
336	    sends $P$ to the identity of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$. It is universal among morphisms $f \colon C \to A$ to smooth proper geometrically irreducible $k$-group schemes $A$ with $f(P) = \eta_A$: this is the classical Albanese property of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$, proved by reducing to the case $k = \overline k$ (Galois descent), where it is a standard consequence of the autoduality of the Jacobian and the fact that line bundles on $C \times A$ trivialised along $\{P\} \times A$ correspond bijectively to morphisms $C \to A^{\vee} = A$ sending $P$ to $\eta_A$.
337	  \end{itemize}
338	
339	  \emph{Mathlib status for Route A.} Each sub-step is missing.
340	  \begin{itemize}
341	    \item Sub-step A.1 requires a working theory of relative line bundles on a product $C \times_k T$ and the descent of the abelian-group structure on $\Pic$ along $\pi^*$. The pieces are present individually but not assembled. (See per-sub-phase budget below: $\sim 700$--$1100$ LOC, $\sim 6$--$10$ iters.)
342	    \item Sub-step A.2 requires FGA representability for $\Pic_{C/k}$, which in turn requires Hilbert / Quot scheme infrastructure not in Mathlib (\texttt{HilbertScheme}, \texttt{QuotScheme}, and the cohomology-and-base-change for proper morphisms with finitely presented kernels). The construction engine for this Quot/Hilbert layer is Nitsure, ``Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes'' (FGA Explained; arXiv:math/0504590), \S\,4 (generic flatness and the flattening stratification) and \S\,5 (the construction of $\mathrm{Quot}$, hence $\mathrm{Hilb}$, as a scheme); see \texttt{references/nitsure-hilbert-quot.md}. (See per-sub-phase budget below: $\sim 2200$--$3000$ LOC, $\sim 15$--$25$ iters --- the dominant Route A block.)
343	    \item Sub-step A.3 requires connectedness machinery for group schemes locally of finite type, the identity-component construction $G^0 \subseteq G$, and the degree map $\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb Z}_k$. (See per-sub-phase budget below: $\sim 600$--$900$ LOC, $\sim 5$--$8$ iters.)
344	    \item Sub-step A.4 requires the universal property of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$, which is the classical Albanese functoriality and is not in Mathlib in this form. (See per-sub-phase budget below: $\sim 900$--$1200$ LOC, $\sim 7$--$11$ iters.)
345	  \end{itemize}
